topics.title: Definitions - Robots, Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definitions - Robots, Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** *Definitions - Robots, Robotics.* This is a foundational, terminology-heavy topic. Examiner focus is on **exact standard definitions (ISO 8373)**, anatomy of a robot, and the difference between a *Robot* and *Robotics*.

## 1.1 What is a Robot? — Formal KTU Definition

The most widely accepted engineering definition of a robot comes from the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, under standard **ISO 8373:2021**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 8373 Definition of an Industrial Robot:**
> *"An **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator**, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

Breaking down the keywords of the definition:

| Keyword | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Automatically controlled** | No continuous human intervention is needed once programmed. |
| **Re-programmable** | Tasks can be changed by altering the written instructions (software), without physically altering the hardware. |
| **Multipurpose** | It is **not** built for a single dedicated task; it can be re-deployed across applications. |
| **Three or more axes** | Has at least **3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** to manipulate objects in 3D space. |
| **Manipulator** | Has a physical mechanism (arm, gripper) to move materials, parts, or tools. |

**Webster's Dictionary** gives a more general, non-industrial definition:
> *"An automatic device that performs functions ordinarily ascribed to human beings (as manipulation, locomotion), or a machine in the form of a human being that performs complex human tasks."*

### 🤖 Conceptual Analogy — The "Helper Arm" Idea

Imagine a factory worker who picks up a hot metal plate from one conveyor belt and places it precisely on another belt, **1,000 times per shift without getting tired or bored**. Now imagine giving that worker:
- A **steel, articulated arm** that never sweats.
- A **brain** (microcontroller) that remembers exactly where to place the plate.
- **Sensors** (eyes and skin) that detect the plate and its temperature.

That mechanical, re-trainable helper is essentially what a **robot** is — a programmable mechanical agent that performs tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously.

---

## 1.2 What is Robotics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of Robotics:**
> *Robotics is the **interdisciplinary branch of mechanical engineering, electronic engineering, computer science, and information engineering** that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robots.*

It is **not** a single machine. It is the **entire science and technology** behind creating, programming, and using robots.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy — Robotics vs. Robot

Think of the relationship between **Robotics** and **Robot** the same way as **"Automotive Engineering"** and **"Car"**:
- A **car** is the physical product.
- **Automotive engineering** is the entire field (fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, electronics, safety) that makes the car exist.

Similarly:
- A **robot** is the physical product.
- **Robotics** is the field covering kinematics, control theory, AI, sensor fusion, mechanics, and ethics that make the robot work.

| Aspect | Robot | Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | A machine / device | A field of study / technology |
| **Scope** | Physical hardware + software | Theory, design, control, application |
| **Example** | ABB IRB 120 industrial arm | The lecture notes of this PEMET748 course! |
| **Lifespan** | A specific object | An evolving discipline since the 1950s |

---

## 1.3 Other KTU-Standard Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Manipulator** | The mechanical arm of a robot, comprising links and joints. |
| **End-Effector** | The tool/gripper attached to the last link of the manipulator (e.g., welding torch, paint sprayer). |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent motions (rotational or translational) a robot can perform. |
| **Pedestal / Base** | The fixed support structure to which the manipulator is anchored. |
| **Teach Pendant** | A handheld control unit used to manually program a robot's positions. |
| **Payload** | The maximum weight a robot can carry while maintaining rated performance, measured in **kg**. |
| **Reach** | The maximum distance from the robot's base to the end-effector, measured in **mm** or **m**. |
| **Repeatability** | The robot's ability to return to a previously taught position, measured in **mm**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** In KTU answer sheets, always quote the **ISO 8373** definition for full marks in 3-mark and 7-mark questions on "Define a Robot". This is the examiner's gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Robotics (KTU Concept)

According to the Robotics Institute of America (RIA), a true industrial robot must exhibit **three or more** of the following four pillars:

1. **Mobility:** Ability to move under its own control (wheels, legs, or arm articulation).
2. **Programmability:** Software-defined task instructions, not hard-wired mechanics.
3. **Sensing:** Ability to perceive the environment (force, vision, proximity sensors).
4. **Automation:** Independent, unattended operation over an extended period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently frame a **3-mark question** as *"List the four D's of Robotics"* or *"What are the three primary characteristics of a robot?"* — Memorize **Mobility, Programmability, and Automation** as the top 3.

---

## 2.2 Brief Historical Context (for Essay-Type 7-Mark Questions)

- **1921:** Czech writer **Karel Čapek** coins the word *"Robot"* in his play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)*, derived from the Slavic word *"robota"* meaning **forced labor**.
- **1954:** **George Devol** files the patent for the first programmable robot (Unimate).
- **1956:** Devol and **Joseph Engelberger** found **Unimation Inc.**, the first robotics company. Engelberger is called the **"Father of Robotics."**
- **1961:** The first Unimate robot is installed at a **General Motors** plant in New Jersey.
- **1942 & 1950:** **Isaac Asimov** formulates the **Three Laws of Robotics** in his short stories.

---

## 2.3 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asimov's Laws (Frequently asked as a 7-mark question):**

1. **Law Zero (added later):** A robot may not harm *humanity*, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.
2. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
3. **Second Law:** A robot must obey orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
4. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Module 1

For this introductory topic, the "formulas" are mostly **performance metrics** and **counting parameters**. No complex calculus is involved at the definition stage.

| Metric / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Definition / Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | $DOF$ | dimensionless integer | Sum of independent translations and rotations. For a planar arm: $DOF = n$ (number of joints in 2D). |
| Number of DOF in 3D space | $DOF_{3D}$ | dimensionless integer | $DOF_{3D} \le 6$ for a fully spatial rigid body (3 translations + 3 rotations). |
| Kutzbach-Grübler Mobility (introductory form) | $M$ | dimensionless | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (for planar mechanisms), where $n$ = links, $j_1$ = lower pairs, $j_2$ = higher pairs. |
| Repeatability | $R_{pt}$ | $\text{mm}$ | The radius of the smallest sphere containing all reached positions over repeated trials. |
| Accuracy | $A_{cc}$ | $\text{mm}$ | The maximum distance between the target position and the average reached position. |
| Payload capacity | $P_{max}$ | $\text{kg}$ | Maximum allowable wrist force at end-effector. |
| Reach / Working Envelope | $R_{each}$ | $\text{mm}$ or $\text{m}$ | Maximum distance from base center to farthest reachable point. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Repeatability** with **Accuracy**. Remember: *Repeatability* = **how close the robot comes to its *own* previous positions** (good robots have low values like $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$). *Accuracy* = **how close the robot comes to the *desired* absolute position** (affected by calibration errors).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Welding, painting, pick-and-place in automotive plants (e.g., FANUC, KUKA).
- **Medicine:** Da Vinci surgical robot performs minimally invasive surgery.
- **Space:** NASA Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) for planetary exploration.
- **Defense & Security:** Bomb disposal robots (DRDO Daksh).
- **Domestic:** Vacuum cleaning robots (iRobot Roomba), warehouse logistics (Amazon Kiva).
- **Agriculture:** Autonomous tractors and fruit-picking drones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by writing *"Robotics is the study of robots"* as a complete answer. The examiner expects you to explicitly mention the **interdisciplinary nature** — *mechanical, electronics, computer science, AI, control systems* — to secure full 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since this is a **definitions-based introductory topic**, the "derivations" are primarily **analytical breakdowns** of key concepts and **proofs of simple counting relationships** that examiners test.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why 6 Degrees of Freedom in 3D Space?

A rigid body in **3-dimensional space** has exactly **6 independent parameters** to fully describe its position and orientation.

### Step 1 — Position in 3D
A point in 3D space requires **3 independent coordinates**:

$$
P_{3D} = (x, y, z)
$$

### Step 2 — Orientation in 3D
To fully orient a rigid body in 3D space, we use **3 independent rotation angles**. The standard convention (Euler angles) is:

$$
R_{xyz}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = R_z(\gamma) \cdot R_y(\beta) \cdot R_x(\alpha)
$$

where the three angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ correspond to rotations about the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes respectively.

### Step 3 — Total Independent Parameters
Adding the translations and rotations:

$$
DOF_{max, 3D} = \underbrace{3}_{\text{translations}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{rotations}} = 6
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Any robot designed to position and orient an object **arbitrarily in 3D space** must have **at least 6 DOF**. A 6-DOF arm is the industry standard for general-purpose manipulation (e.g., the PUMA 560).

### Step 4 — Common Industrial DOF Configurations

| DOF | Typical Robot Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $3$ | Cartesian (gantry) | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| $4$ | SCARA | PCB assembly, packaging |
| $5$ | Palletizing arm | Welding, painting |
| $6$ | Articulated / Anthropomorphic | Welding, machining, complex assembly |
| $\ge 7$ | Redundant manipulator | Avoiding obstacles in cluttered workspaces |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Breakdown — Anatomy of a Robot

The anatomy can be decomposed into **four fundamental subsystems**:

### Subsystem A — Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)
- Series of rigid **links** connected by **joints**.
- Joints are either **Revolute (R)** — rotational motion, or **Prismatic (P)** — linear motion.
- The chain ends in an **End-Effector** (gripper, tool).

### Subsystem B — Drive System (Actuators)
Provides the power to move the joints. Three main types:

$$
\text{Drive Types} = \begin{cases} \text{Electric} & : \text{Servo motors, stepper motors} \\ \text{Hydraulic} & : \text{High payload (e.g., 1000+ kg)} \\ \text{Pneumatic} & : \text{Lightweight, fast pick-and-place} \end{cases}
$$

### Subsystem C — Sensing System (Sensors)
Two broad categories:

$$
\text{Sensors} = \begin{cases} \text{Internal (proprioceptive)} & : \text{Encoders, resolvers, tachometers} \\ \text{External (exteroceptive)} & : \text{Cameras, force/torque, proximity, range finders} \end{cases}
$$

### Subsystem D — Control System (Controller + Software)
- **Controller:** A microcomputer (e.g., PLC, ARM Cortex, industrial PC) that runs the trajectory and feedback loops.
- **Software:** Programs written in robot-specific languages (e.g., **VAL II**, **RAPID**, **KRL**) or generic languages (Python, C++) using ROS (Robot Operating System).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Explain the four components of a robot"*, students often miss **the control system**. The most common model answer lists: **Manipulator, End-Effector, Actuator with Drive, and Controller with Sensor Feedback** — note the closed-loop feedback nature, which is mandatory for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 3-Mark Pitfalls

| Student Frequently Writes | What Examiner Expects (Correct) | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|
| *"A robot is a machine that looks like a human."* | ISO 8373: *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator* | Up to **2 marks** |
| *"Robotics = study of robots."* | Robotics = *interdisciplinary field of ME, ECE, CSE for design, control, and application of robots* | Up to **2 marks** |
| *"Repeatability and accuracy are the same."* | Repeatability = *closeness to its own previous positions*; Accuracy = *closeness to desired position* | Up to **1 mark** |
| *"Asimov's first law is about robot's safety."* | First law: *A robot may not injure a human being or allow harm through inaction.* | Up to **1 mark** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robot

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a general-purpose industrial robot, showing how the four subsystems interact in a **closed-loop control flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER / OPERATOR] -->|Teach Pendant or HMI| B[CENTRAL CONTROLLER]
    B -->|Trajectory Commands| C[DRIVE SYSTEM ACTUATORS]
    C -->|Torque and Motion| D[MECHANICAL MANIPULATOR LINKS AND JOINTS]
    D -->|Physical Motion| E[END EFFECTOR GRIPPER OR TOOL]
    E -->|Performs Task On| F[WORKPIECE OR ENVIRONMENT]
    
    G[INTERNAL SENSORS] -->|Joint Position and Velocity Feedback| B
    H[EXTERNAL SENSORS] -->|Vision Force Proximity Data| B
    
    B -.->|Corrective Control Signal| C
    
    classDef ctrl fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#1a365d,color:#ffffff
    classDef act fill:#c05621,stroke:#7b341e,color:#ffffff
    classDef mech fill:#2f855a,stroke:#22543d,color:#ffffff
    classDef sen fill:#805ad5,stroke:#44337a,color:#ffffff
    
    class B ctrl
    class C act
    class D,E mech
    class G,H sen
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The blue **Controller** is the brain. It sends motion commands to the orange **Actuators**, which move the green **Mechanical Manipulator**. The green **End-Effector** interacts with the physical world. The purple **Sensors** feed back actual joint positions and environmental data to the controller, closing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Relationship — Robot vs. Robotics

This diagram shows the **relationship between a single robot** and the **broader robotics ecosystem** that surrounds it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[ROBOT The Physical Machine]
    R -->|Used In| App1[Manufacturing]
    R -->|Used In| App2[Medicine]
    R -->|Used In| App3[Space Exploration]
    R -->|Used In| App4[Domestic Service]
    
    RB[ROBOTICS The Science and Field]
    RB -->|Includes| Sub1[Mechanical Design]
    RB -->|Includes| Sub2[Control Theory]
    RB -->|Includes| Sub3[Artificial Intelligence]
    RB -->|Includes| Sub4[Sensor Fusion]
    
    RB -->|Enables the Creation Of| R
    
    classDef robot fill:#dd6b20,stroke:#7b341e,color:#ffffff
    classDef robo fill:#3182ce,stroke:#1a365d,color:#ffffff
    
    class R robot
    class RB robo
```

---

## 4.3 Classification of Robots (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CLASSIFICATION OF ROBOTS]
    
    A --> B[By Generation]
    B --> B1[1st Gen - Playback]
    B --> B2[2nd Gen - Sensory]
    B --> B3[3rd Gen - Intelligent with AI]
    
    A --> C[By Geometry]
    C --> C1[Cartesian or Gantry]
    C --> C2[Cylindrical]
    C --> C3[Polar or Spherical]
    C --> C4[SCARA]
    C --> C5[Articulated or Anthropomorphic]
    
    A --> D[By Application]
    D --> D1[Industrial Manipulator]
    D --> D2[Mobile Robot]
    D --> D3[Humanoid]
    D --> D4[Autonomous Vehicle]
    
    A --> E[By Drive System]
    E --> E1[Electric]
    E --> E2[Hydraulic]
    E --> E3[Pneumatic]
    
    classDef gen fill:#38b2ac,stroke:#234e52,color:#ffffff
    classDef geo fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#744210,color:#ffffff
    classDef app fill:#e53e3e,stroke:#742a2a,color:#ffffff
    classDef drv fill:#805ad5,stroke:#44337a,color:#ffffff
    
    class B,B1,B2,B3 gen
    class C,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 geo
    class D,D1,D2,D3,D4 app
    class E,E1,E2,E3 drv
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short answer, **3 marks** each, typically 2 questions per module.
> - Part B: Long answer, **14 marks** each, with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B.
> - Each 14-mark question is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a robot as per ISO 8373 standard.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373)**, a robot is defined as:
> *"An automatically controlled, **re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator**, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*
>
> The three essential keywords are: **(i)** automatically controlled, **(ii)** re-programmable, and **(iii)** multipurpose. A robot must also possess at least **3 degrees of freedom (axes)** to qualify under this standard.

*(Examiner awards full 3 marks for the exact ISO phrasing plus the keyword breakdown.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a Robot and Robotics.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Robot | Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | It is a **physical machine/device**. | It is a **field of study and engineering discipline**. |
| 2 | Scope is limited to one machine. | Scope spans mechanical, electronic, computer science, and AI domains. |
| 3 | Example: ABB IRB 120 industrial arm. | Example: The PEMET748 B.Tech course syllabus. |
| 4 | Tangible product of engineering. | The science behind creating, controlling, and applying the product. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **anatomy of a robot** with a neat **functional block diagram**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The anatomy of a robot consists of **four major functional subsystems**, working together in a closed-loop manner:

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator):**
   A series of rigid **links** connected by **joints**. Joints are either **Revolute (R)** providing rotation or **Prismatic (P)** providing linear sliding. The chain terminates in an **End-Effector** such as a gripper or welding torch.

2. **Drive / Actuation System:**
   Provides the energy to move the joints. Types include:
   - **Electric:** Servo and stepper motors (most common, precise).
   - **Hydraulic:** High payload capacity (used in heavy industry).
   - **Pneumatic:** Lightweight and fast (used in pick-and-place).

3. **Sensing System:**
   - **Internal sensors:** Encoders, resolvers — measure joint angle and velocity.
   - **External sensors:** Cameras, force/torque sensors, ultrasonic range finders — perceive the environment.
   The sensor feedback is **fed back to the controller** to close the control loop.

4. **Control System:**
   A digital controller (microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC) executes the programmed trajectory. It compares the **desired position** with the **actual sensor feedback** and computes the **corrective signal** sent to the actuators.

**Block Diagram:**

$$
\boxed{\text{User}} \xrightarrow{\text{Program}} \boxed{\text{Controller}} \xrightarrow{\text{Command}} \boxed{\text{Actuator}} \xrightarrow{\text{Motion}} \boxed{\text{Manipulator}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{End-Effector}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Sensors}} \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} \boxed{\text{Controller}}
$$

*(Full 7 marks: 4 for explanation + 3 for the labelled diagram.)*

---

**(b)** Classify robots based on **geometry**, **generation**, and **drive system**. Give one industrial application for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**By Geometry:**

| Type | Structure | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Gantry (PPP)** | 3 prismatic axes along X, Y, Z | CNC loading, 3D printing |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | 1 revolute + 2 prismatic | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Polar / Spherical (RRP)** | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic | Die casting, forging |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic, selective compliance | PCB assembly, packaging |
| **Articulated / Anthropomorphic (RRR)** | All revolute joints | Welding, painting, machining |

**By Generation:**

- **1st Generation (Playback):** Simple pre-programmed repetitive motion, no sensors. Used in spot welding.
- **2nd Generation (Sensory):** Uses force/vision sensors for adaptive control. Used in arc welding with seam tracking.
- **3rd Generation (Intelligent):** Incorporates AI and machine learning for autonomous decision-making. Used in self-driving cars, humanoid robots.

**By Drive System:**

- **Electric:** Precision assembly, clean rooms. Example: Epson SCARA.
- **Hydraulic:** Heavy payload. Example: Caterpillar mining excavators.
- **Pneumatic:** Low-cost pick-and-place. Example: Food packaging industry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define **Robotics**. Explain in detail the **four basic components of any robotic system**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Definition:** Robotics is the *interdisciplinary branch of mechanical engineering, electronic engineering, computer science, and information engineering* that deals with the **design, construction, operation, and application of robots**.

The four basic components are:

1. **Manipulator / Mechanical Arm:** The skeletal structure with links and joints.
2. **End-Effector:** The tool or gripper at the arm's tip (e.g., welding gun, suction cup).
3. **Power Supply / Drive System:** Electric, hydraulic, or pneumatic actuators that provide motion.
4. **Controller:** The "brain" — a computer that stores the program, processes sensor inputs, and generates actuator commands.

---

**(b)** State and explain **Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics**. Discuss their significance in modern robot ethics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Issued by science-fiction author **Isaac Asimov** in his 1942 short story *"Runaround"*, the three laws are:

1. **First Law:** *A robot may not injure a human being, or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.*

2. **Second Law:** *A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.*

3. **Third Law:** *A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.*

4. **Law Zero (added later, 1985):** *A robot may not harm humanity, or, through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.*

**Significance in Modern Ethics:**
- Provides a foundational ethical framework for **AI safety**, **autonomous weapons**, and **driverless cars**.
- Forms the basis of modern discussions like **Asilomar AI Principles** (2017).
- Highlights the **conflict of priorities**: When a self-driving car must choose between hitting a pedestrian or its own passenger, Asimov's laws help reason about the dilemma.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks on Robot Definitions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ISO 8373 reference:** A 3-mark "Define a robot" question requires the **exact ISO phrasing**. Writing *"a robot is a machine that does work automatically"* will fetch at most 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Conflating Robot and Robotics:** Many students write *"Robotics is the study of robots"* without listing the **interdisciplinary nature** (Mechanical + Electronics + Computer Science + AI). Examiner deducts up to 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Swapping Repeatability and Accuracy:** Examiners specifically test this. Memorize: **Repeatability = closeness to *itself*; Accuracy = closeness to *target*.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a **labelled block diagram** is mandatory. Writing only theory without any figure costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Asimov's law misquote:** Always state them **in order** (First → Second → Third) and mention **Law Zero** for bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Definitions: Robots, Robotics**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Robot (ISO 8373):** *automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in 3 or more axes.*
- **Robotics:** *Interdisciplinary field (ME + ECE + CSE + AI) of design, construction, operation, and application of robots.*
- **Father of Robotics:** Joseph Engelberger.
- **Origin of the word:** Karel Čapek (1921), from Slavic *robota* = forced labor.

### ✅ Key Performance Metrics
- **DOF** $\le 6$ in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Repeatability** $=$ closeness to *own* previous position.
- **Accuracy** $=$ closeness to *desired* position.
- **Payload** $=$ max weight (kg).
- **Reach** $=$ max distance (mm).

### ✅ Four Subsystems
1. **Mechanical Manipulator** (links + joints).
2. **Drive System** (electric / hydraulic / pneumatic).
3. **Sensors** (internal + external).
4. **Controller** (closed-loop feedback).

### ✅ Asimov's Laws (in order)
1. Don't harm a human.
2. Obey humans (unless conflicts with 1).
3. Protect itself (unless conflicts with 1 or 2).
4. **Law Zero (1985):** Don't harm humanity.

### ✅ Five Geometry Types
Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Polar/Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP), Articulated (RRR).

### ✅ Examiner's Golden Rule
> Always quote the **ISO 8373 standard**, label every **block diagram**, and explicitly mention **interdisciplinary nature** for Robotics — these three habits alone can lift your score by **8 to 10 marks** across the full semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled and Legged Robots, Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robot? (KTU Formal Definition)

A **Robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional, computer-controlled manipulator or device designed to move materials, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. The **Robot Institute of America (RIA)** defines an industrial robot as *"a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices, through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Robots are classified based on their mechanical structure, intended environment, and degree of autonomy. The three principal categories required at the B.Tech level are **Manipulators**, **Mobile Robots**, and **Aerial Robots**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Three Faces of a Robot

Think of a robot as a *digital employee*:

- A **Manipulator** is like a *factory worker's arm bolted to a table*. It cannot walk, but its hand can reach anywhere within its workspace. Examples: A welding arm in a car factory, a surgical robot in an operation theatre.
- A **Mobile Robot** is like a *delivery boy on wheels or legs*. It can roam around the building, avoid obstacles, and carry loads. Examples: A vacuum-cleaning robot (wheels), a Boston Dynamics Spot dog (legs).
- An **Aerial Robot** is like a *drone courier*. It operates in 3D air space. Examples: A quadcopter filming a wedding, an agricultural drone spraying pesticides.

## 1.3 Classification of Robots (KTU Board Taxonomy)

| Category | Sub-Type | Distinguishing Feature | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manipulator (Industrial)** | Articulated, Cartesian, SCARA, Cylindrical, Spherical, Parallel | Fixed base, serial/parallel chain of links | KUKA KR 60, ABB IRB |
| **Mobile Robot** | Wheeled (WMR), Legged (LMR) | Mobile base, navigates ground | AGV, Mars Rover, Spot |
| **Aerial Robot** | Quadrotor, Helicopter, Fixed-wing, Blimp | Aerial locomotion using lift/thrust | DJI Phantom, MQ-9 Reaper |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid robots** (e.g., a manipulator mounted on a mobile base, or a flying manipulator drone) are an active research frontier combining two or more categories.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Board Exams

This is a **breadth-first foundation topic**. The questions test whether the student can:

- **Recall (L1)** the names and configurations.
- **Understand (L2)** why a particular robot type is chosen for a given task.
- **Apply (L3)** Grübler's formula to determine Degrees of Freedom (DOF).
- **Analyze (L4)** the stability and locomotion of legged/wheeled platforms.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manipulators — Anatomy and Configurations

A manipulator is a **serial or parallel chain of rigid links** connected by joints (revolute R or prismatic P), anchored at a fixed base. Its anatomy consists of:

1. **Base / Pedestal** — fixed reference frame.
2. **Links** — rigid bodies that transmit motion.
3. **Joints** — relative motion generators. There are **5 lower-pair joints** and several higher-pair joints. Common lower-pair joints:
   - **Revolute (R)** — 1 DOF, rotation.
   - **Prismatic (P)** — 1 DOF, translation.
   - **Helical, Cylindrical, Spherical, Planar, Universal** — multi-DOF joints.
4. **End-Effector** — gripper, welding torch, suction cup, etc.
5. **Workspace** — the reachable 3D volume of the end-effector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Configuration Naming Convention:** Write joint symbols from base to end-effector. Example: **RRR** = three-revolute articulated arm.

### The Six Standard Manipulator Geometries

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Gantry** | PPP | Rectangular cuboid | Pick-and-place, CNC, 3D printers |
| **Cylindrical** | RPP | Hollow cylinder | Loading CNC lathes |
| **Spherical / Polar** | RRP | Hollow sphere | Die-casting, forging |
| **SCARA** | RRP (Selective Compliance) | Flat cylindrical disk | PCB assembly, packaging |
| **Articulated / Anthropomorphic** | RRR (often 6R) | Sphere-like | Welding, painting, assembly |
| **Parallel (Delta)** | Triangular base + 3 parallel arms | Dome / hemisphere | High-speed pick-and-place |

### Manipulator DOF — Grübler's Formula

The number of degrees of freedom of a planar or spatial mechanism is given by **Grübler's formula**:

$$
M \;=\; d\,(n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{J}\,(d - f_i)
$$

where:

- $M$ = mobility (number of DOF) of the mechanism,
- $d$ = number of links minus 1 degrees of freedom in the unconstrained space (**d = 3** planar, **d = 6** spatial),
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground),
- $J$ = total number of joints,
- $f_i$ = number of DOF of the $i^{\text{th}}$ joint.

For **all joints being lower pairs (1 DOF)** in a spatial chain, the simplified form becomes:

$$
M \;=\; 6\,(n-1) \;-\; 5J_1 \;-\; 4J_2 \;-\; 3J_3 \;-\; 2J_4 \;-\; J_5
$$

where $J_k$ is the number of joints with $k$ DOF.

## 2.2 Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR)

A WMR uses **wheels** for locomotion. The choice of wheel arrangement defines its maneuverability, controllability, and stability.

### 2.2.1 Wheel Types

- **Standard (fixed) wheel** — 1 DOF (rotation about axle).
- **Caster wheel** — 2 DOF (rotation + steering).
- **Swedish (omnidirectional) wheel** — 3 DOF (small passive rollers on periphery allow lateral motion).
- **Ball / spherical wheel** — omnidirectional.

### 2.2.2 Wheeled Robot Drive Configurations

| Drive Type | Drive Wheels | Steering | Maneuverability | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential Drive** | 2 independent wheels | None | Turn in place (zero turning radius) | Roomba, most research WMRs |
| **Ackermann / Car-Like** | 2 (rear) | 2 (front) steered | Car-like; non-holonomic with bounded turning radius | Autonomous cars, ATRV |
| **Synchronous (Synchro) Drive** | 3 or 4 wheels driven together | All rotate together | Rotate and translate simultaneously | HelpMate hospital robot |
| **Tricycle** | 1 driven | 2 steered | Ackermann-like; 1-DOF steering | Classic research WMRs |
| **Omnidirectional** | ≥3 Swedish/ball wheels | Independent | Move in any planar direction | KUKA youBot, RoboCup SSL |

### 2.2.3 Differential-Drive Kinematics (KTU High-Yield)

For a differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r$, wheel separation $b$ (distance between the two drive wheels), and right/left wheel angular velocities $\omega_R, \omega_L$:

$$
v_R = r\,\omega_R \quad,\quad v_L = r\,\omega_L
$$

The robot's linear and angular velocities are:

$$
v \;=\; \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \;=\; \frac{r\,(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} \;=\; \frac{r\,(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is located on the wheelbase line at a distance:

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega} \;=\; \frac{b\,(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2\,(\omega_R - \omega_L)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A differential-drive robot is **non-holonomic** — it cannot move sideways directly; it must perform a maneuver (carrot-and-stick / parallel-park motion) to translate laterally.

### 2.2.4 Ackermann Steering Geometry

For a car-like vehicle with wheelbase $L$ and steering angle $\delta$ of the front wheel, the turning radius is:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{L}{\tan\delta}
$$

## 2.3 Legged Mobile Robots (LMR)

Legged robots use **discrete footholds** to traverse uneven terrain. The number of legs strongly affects stability and control complexity.

### 2.3.1 Static vs. Dynamic Stability

- **Static stability**: The robot's **Center of Gravity (CoG)** projection on the ground remains within the **support polygon** formed by the supporting feet at all times — works only if $n \geq 3$.
- **Dynamic stability**: The robot is allowed to fall during part of the gait, as long as it regains balance — works for $n \geq 2$ (humanoid walking).

### 2.3.2 Number of Legs and Gait Properties

| $n$ Legs | Static Stability | Common Gait | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Impossible (always falls) | Hopping | Salto robot |
| 2 | Marginal (dynamic only) | Walking / running | Cassie, Atlas, ASIMO |
| 4 | Stable (≥3 feet always) | Crawl, Pace, Trot, Bound | Spot, ANYmal, BigDog |
| 6 | Highly stable, fault-tolerant | Tripod (alternating 3-3) | Most research hexapods |
| 8 | Maximum stability | Wave gait | BigHex, search-rescue bots |

### 2.3.3 Support Polygon and Static Stability Margin

For a quadruped standing on 3 feet, the support polygon is a triangle. The **Static Stability Margin (SSM)** is the perpendicular distance from the CoG projection to the nearest edge of the polygon. For a square stance of side $a$, the SSM is $a/2$.

## 2.4 Aerial Robots (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles — UAVs)

Aerial robots exploit aerodynamic forces (lift, thrust, drag) to fly. They are classified by:

- **Lift mechanism** (rotary-wing vs. fixed-wing vs. lighter-than-air).
- **Autonomy level** (teleoperated, semi-autonomous, fully autonomous).
- **Application domain** (military, civil, commercial).

### 2.4.1 Common Aerial Robot Types

| Type | Lift Principle | Hover | Maneuver | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quadrotor / Quadcopter** | 4 fixed-pitch rotors | Yes | Excellent, 6-DOF control | DJI Phantom, Parrot AR |
| **Single-Rotor Helicopter** | 1 main + 1 tail rotor | Yes | High efficiency | Yamaha RMAX |
| **Fixed-Wing UAV** | Forward airspeed over wings | No | Long range, high speed | MQ-9 Reaper, Cessna 172 UAV |
| **Hybrid VTOL** | Tilting rotors / thrust-vectoring | Yes | Combines hover + range | Bell V-247 Vigilant |
| **Blimp / Lighter-Than-Air** | Buoyancy from lifting gas | Yes | Low energy, slow | Airborne drones for inspection |

### 2.4.2 Quadrotor Dynamics (KTU High-Yield)

A quadrotor has **4 fixed-pitch propellers** mounted at the ends of a rigid cross-frame. Motors $M_1$ and $M_3$ (diagonal pair) rotate clockwise; $M_2$ and $M_4$ rotate counter-clockwise (to balance reactive torque).

Let $T_i$ be the thrust produced by motor $i$ and $c$ be a motor constant. Define:

$$
T \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i \quad\text{(total upward thrust)}
$$

$$
\tau_\phi \;=\; b\,(T_2 - T_4) \quad\text{(roll torque about } x\text{-axis)}
$$

$$
\tau_\theta \;=\; b\,(T_3 - T_1) \quad\text{(pitch torque about } y\text{-axis)}
$$

$$
\tau_\psi \;=\; d\,(\Omega_1^2 - \Omega_2^2 + \Omega_3^2 - \Omega_4^2) \quad\text{(yaw torque about } z\text{-axis)}
$$

where $b$ is the distance from the rotor to the CoG, $d$ is a drag coefficient, and $\Omega_i$ is the $i^{\text{th}}$ rotor's angular speed.

### 2.4.3 Equations of Motion (Newton-Euler)

For a quadrotor of mass $m$ and inertia matrix $\mathbf{I} \in \mathbb{R}^{3\times 3}$, with body angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [p, q, r]^{\top}$:

$$
m\,\ddot{\mathbf{r}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ -mg \end{bmatrix} \;+\; \mathbf{R}\,\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ T \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\mathbf{I}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\tau} \;-\; \boldsymbol{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\,\boldsymbol{\omega})
$$

where $\mathbf{r}$ is the position of the CoG in the inertial frame and $\mathbf{R}$ is the rotation matrix from body to inertial frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Under-actuation property**: A quadrotor has **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation) but only **4 independent control inputs** ($T, \tau_\phi, \tau_\theta, \tau_\psi$). Hence it is **under-actuated** — it cannot translate laterally directly; it must *tilt* first.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grübler mobility | $M = 6(n-1) - \sum_i (6 - f_i)$ | $n$ = links, $f_i$ = joint DOF |
| 2 | Differential-drive linear velocity | $v = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$ | $r$ wheel radius |
| 3 | Differential-drive angular velocity | $\omega = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}$ | $b$ wheel separation |
| 4 | ICR radius | $R_{\text{ICR}} = v / \omega$ | sign indicates side |
| 5 | Ackermann radius | $R = L / \tan\delta$ | $L$ wheelbase, $\delta$ steer angle |
| 6 | Quadrotor total thrust | $T = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$ | acts along body $z$ |
| 7 | Quadrotor roll torque | $\tau_\phi = b(T_2 - T_4)$ | about body $x$ |
| 8 | Quadrotor pitch torque | $\tau_\theta = b(T_3 - T_1)$ | about body $y$ |
| 9 | Quadrotor yaw torque | $\tau_\psi = d(\Omega_1^2 - \Omega_2^2 + \Omega_3^2 - \Omega_4^2)$ | about body $z$ |
| 10 | Static stability condition | $\text{CoG projection} \in \text{support polygon}$ | requires $n \geq 3$ legs |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manipulators** dominate **Industry 4.0** smart factories (Tesla, Foxconn assembly lines).
- **Wheeled robots** are the workhorses of **warehouse logistics** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) and **planetary exploration** (NASA Perseverance rover).
- **Legged robots** are critical for **disaster response, military reconnaissance, and forestry** where wheeled locomotion fails on uneven terrain.
- **Aerial robots** enable **precision agriculture, last-mile delivery (Zipline), aerial cinematography, and infrastructure inspection**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degrees of Freedom of a 6R Articulated Manipulator

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** Compute the DOF of an articulated manipulator consisting of 6 revolute joints and 7 links (1 ground + 6 moving links).

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.** For a spatial mechanism:

- $d = 6$ (spatial — 3 translations + 3 rotations)
- $n = 7$ links
- $J_1 = 6$ joints, each with $f_i = 1$ DOF (revolute).

**Step 2 — Substitute into Grübler's formula.**

$$
M \;=\; d\,(n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{J}(d - f_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Plug the numerical values.**

$$
M \;=\; 6\,(7 - 1) \;-\; 6\,(6 - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
M \;=\; 6 \cdot 6 \;-\; 6 \cdot 5 \;=\; 36 \;-\; 30 \;=\; 6
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The 6R articulated arm has **6 DOF**, fully spanning 3D position (3) and orientation (3) of the end-effector. This is the most common configuration for general-purpose industrial robots like the **KUKA KR 60**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential-Drive Kinematics from First Principles

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A differential-drive WMR has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m and wheel separation $b = 0.30$ m. If the left and right wheels are driven at $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s respectively, compute the linear velocity, angular velocity, and ICR radius.

**Step 1 — Wheel contact velocities.**

$$
v_L \;=\; r\,\omega_L \;=\; 0.05 \times 10 \;=\; 0.50 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_R \;=\; r\,\omega_R \;=\; 0.05 \times 12 \;=\; 0.60 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity of robot CoG.**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.60 + 0.50}{2} \;=\; 0.55 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity.**

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{v_R - v_L}{b} \;=\; \frac{0.60 - 0.50}{0.30} \;=\; 0.333 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 4 — ICR radius.**

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega} \;=\; \frac{0.55}{0.333} \;=\; 1.65 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Direction.** Since $v_R > v_L$, the robot turns **left** (CCW about an ICR 1.65 m to the left of the midpoint).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case:** If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$, then $v = 0$ but $\omega \neq 0$ — the robot **spins in place**. This is a unique advantage of differential-drive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A car-like WMR has a wheelbase $L = 1.2$ m. To negotiate a corridor of width 2 m, what steering angle $\delta$ is required?

**Step 1 — Required turning radius.** For the robot to pivot around the centre of the corridor, $R \geq$ half-width + robot half-width. Assuming robot half-width $\approx 0.3$ m and pivot at the centre line: $R = 1.0$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply Ackermann formula.**

$$
\tan\delta \;=\; \frac{L}{R} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{1.0} \;=\; 1.2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute angle.**

$$
\delta \;=\; \arctan(1.2) \;\approx\; 50.2^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Reality check.** Steeper than 45° — Ackermann cars cannot make sharp turns. This is why indoor differential drives are preferred over Ackermann.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quadrotor Hover and Altitude Dynamics

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A quadrotor of mass $m = 1.5$ kg has 4 rotors each producing maximum thrust $T_{\max} = 5$ N. Find the maximum vertical acceleration at hover and the thrust required to climb at $a_z = 1$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Hover condition.** At hover, total thrust equals weight.

$$
T_{\text{hover}} \;=\; m\,g \;=\; 1.5 \times 9.81 \;=\; 14.715 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum available thrust.**

$$
T_{\max,\text{total}} \;=\; 4 \times 5 \;=\; 20 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum net upward force.**

$$
F_{\text{net,max}} \;=\; T_{\max,\text{total}} - m\,g \;=\; 20 - 14.715 \;=\; 5.285 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum vertical acceleration.**

$$
a_{z,\max} \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{net,max}}}{m} \;=\; \frac{5.285}{1.5} \;\approx\; 3.52 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Thrust for $a_z = 1$ m/s² upward.**

$$
T_{\text{req}} \;=\; m\,(g + a_z) \;=\; 1.5 \times (9.81 + 1) \;=\; 16.215 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Per-rotor thrust share.** Since all four contribute equally in pure vertical motion:

$$
T_i \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{req}}}{4} \;=\; 4.054 \text{ N per rotor}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Thrust margin** = $(T_{\max,\text{total}} - T_{\text{hover}}) / T_{\text{hover}} \times 100\% = 35.9\%$. A healthy margin allows for payload, wind disturbances, and aggressive maneuvers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Robot

| Application Need | Recommended Robot Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| High-precision PCB pick-and-place | SCARA manipulator | Selective compliance, fast in horizontal plane |
| Welding car chassis | 6R articulated manipulator | Spherical workspace, 6 DOF |
| Warehouse parcel sorting | Differential-drive WMR | Flat floor, holonomic-style rotation |
| Exploration of Mars terrain | Rocker-bogie 6-wheel WMR | Rough terrain, large wheels for obstacles |
| Forestry inspection | Quadruped (e.g., Spot) | Legs adapt to roots, logs, slopes |
| Crop monitoring in 10,000-acre farm | Fixed-wing UAV | Long endurance, large area coverage |
| Indoor surveillance | Quadrotor | Hover capability, agile maneuvers |
| Bridge crack inspection | Hybrid: manipulator on WMR | Reach + mobility combine |
| Bomb disposal | Tracked manipulator on mobile base | Tracks + arm for hazardous materials |

## 4.3 Differential-Drive Kinematic Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    In2[omega L - Left wheel speed]:::input --> Mix1

    SumV --> DivV[Divide by 2]:::calc
    DivV --> OutV[Linear velocity v]:::output

    Mix1 --> DiffV[Difference V_R minus V_L]:::calc
    DiffV --> DivW[Divide by b - wheelbase]:::calc
    DivW --> OutW[Angular velocity omega]:::output

    OutV --> DivICR[Divide v by omega]:::calc
    OutW --> DivICR
    DivICR --> OutR[ICR radius R]:::output

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: Differentiate between a serial manipulator and a parallel manipulator with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Serial Manipulator | Parallel Manipulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Open kinematic chain — links in series | Closed kinematic chain — multiple arms share a common end-effector |
| **Workspace** | Large, irregular | Small, dome-shaped |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (errors accumulate link-by-link) | Higher (errors average out) |
| **Payload** | Lower | Higher (load shared) |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Very high |
| **Examples** | KUKA KR 60 (6R articulated), ABB IRB | Delta robot, Stewart platform, Hexapod |

A serial manipulator has **links connected end-to-end**, while a parallel manipulator has **multiple independent limbs supporting a single moving platform**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: Why is a quadrotor said to be under-actuated? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

A quadrotor has **6 degrees of freedom** in 3D space (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw) but possesses only **4 independent control inputs** — the four rotor speeds $\Omega_1, \Omega_2, \Omega_3, \Omega_4$.

- The **total thrust $T$** controls vertical motion (heave).
- Differential thrusts control **roll $\phi$, pitch $\theta$, yaw $\psi$**.

However, there is no dedicated actuator for **lateral translation (sway)** or **forward translation (surge)**. To move sideways, the quadrotor must **first tilt** (a rotational motion), which then produces a horizontal acceleration component due to the tilted thrust vector. Hence, 4 inputs cannot independently command 6 DOF — it is **under-actuated**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of manipulators based on mechanical configuration with neat sketches of each. Discuss their workspaces and typical industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m and wheel separation $b = 0.40$ m. The robot is commanded to follow a circular path of radius 2 m. Find the required angular velocities of the left and right wheels. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The six standard manipulator configurations are:

1. **Cartesian (PPP)** — Three prismatic joints along orthogonal $X, Y, Z$ axes. Workspace: rectangular cuboid. Applications: CNC machines, gantry pick-and-place, 3D printers.
2. **Cylindrical (RPP)** — Revolute about base, two prismatic. Workspace: hollow cylinder. Applications: loading/unloading lathes.
3. **Spherical/Polar (RRP)** — Two revolute + one prismatic. Workspace: hollow sphere. Applications: forging, die-casting.
4. **SCARA (RRP)** — Two revolute + one prismatic, all axes vertical. Workspace: flat circular disk. Applications: PCB assembly, packaging.
5. **Articulated (RRR)** — Three revolute joints. Workspace: sphere-like. Applications: welding, painting, sealing.
6. **Parallel (Delta)** — Three parallel arms connected to a common platform. Workspace: hemispherical dome. Applications: high-speed packaging.

The **workspace** of each configuration depends on the geometry of its joints. The choice is driven by **reach, payload, repeatability, and cost**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute angular velocity of robot.**

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{v}{R}
$$

where $v$ is linear velocity (we need to express in terms of wheel speeds).

**Step 2 — Express in terms of wheel angular velocities.**

Recall:

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{b}
$$

**Step 3 — Express linear velocity $v$ in terms of wheels.**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Set up turning-radius relation.**

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega} \;=\; \frac{b\,(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2(\omega_R - \omega_L)}
$$

Given $R_{\text{ICR}} = 2$ m, $b = 0.40$ m, $r = 0.08$ m. The turning radius relation is independent of $r$ — we need an additional constraint. Usually, the **average forward speed** $\bar{\omega}$ is given; assume $\bar{\omega} = (\omega_R + \omega_L)/2 = 20$ rad/s.

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$
2 \;=\; \frac{0.40 \cdot (2 \cdot 20)}{2\,(\omega_R - \omega_L)} \;=\; \frac{16}{\omega_R - \omega_L}
$$

$$
\omega_R - \omega_L \;=\; \frac{16}{2} \;=\; 8 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve simultaneous equations.**

$$
\omega_R + \omega_L \;=\; 40 \quad,\quad \omega_R - \omega_L \;=\; 8
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; \omega_R \;=\; \frac{40 + 8}{2} \;=\; 24 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; \omega_L \;=\; \frac{40 - 8}{2} \;=\; 16 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify.**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{0.08 \times (24 + 16)}{2} \;=\; \frac{0.08 \times 40}{2} \;=\; 1.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{0.08 \times (24 - 16)}{0.40} \;=\; \frac{0.08 \times 8}{0.40} \;=\; 1.6 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
R_{\text{ICR}} \;=\; \frac{1.6}{1.6} \;=\; 1 \text{ m?}
$$

Re-check — with corrected assumption $\bar{\omega} = 25$ rad/s:

$$
\omega_R + \omega_L \;=\; 50
$$

$$
\omega_R - \omega_L \;=\; \frac{b \cdot (\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2 R_{\text{ICR}}} \;=\; \frac{0.40 \times 50}{4} \;=\; 5
$$

$$
\omega_R = 27.5 \text{ rad/s},\quad \omega_L = 22.5 \text{ rad/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse $R_{\text{ICR}}$ with the distance between the wheels. The ICR is computed as $v / \omega$. Without a second constraint (like mean speed), the problem is under-determined. Always **read carefully**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** Compare wheeled and legged mobile robots. Discuss their advantages, limitations, and applications. With neat diagrams, explain the tripod gait of a hexapod robot. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A quadrotor of mass $m = 2$ kg has 4 rotors, each capable of producing a maximum thrust of 8 N. Compute (i) the maximum vertical acceleration at hover, (ii) the roll torque if motors 2 and 4 produce thrust differing by 2 N (distance $b = 0.25$ m), and (iii) whether the quadrotor can climb at $a_z = 2$ m/s² with a 1 kg payload. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR) | Legged Mobile Robot (LMR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy efficiency on flat ground** | High (rolling friction is low) | Low (lift cycle, joint actuation) |
| **Performance on uneven terrain** | Poor (wheels lose contact) | Excellent (discrete footholds) |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Simple (fewer actuators) | Complex (many joints, sensors per leg) |
| **Control complexity** | Moderate (kinematic model) | High (gait planning, balance) |
| **Stability** | Inherently stable | Requires active control for $n < 6$ |
| **Speed (flat)** | High (m/s to km/h) | Moderate (typically m/s) |
| **Examples** | Mars Rover, Roomba, AGVs | Spot, ANYmal, Cassie |

**Tripod Gait of a Hexapod:**

In a hexapod (6 legs), the legs are divided into two alternating tripods:

- **Tripod A**: Front-left (FL), Middle-right (MR), Hind-left (HL)
- **Tripod B**: Front-right (FR), Middle-left (ML), Hind-right (HR)

In each gait cycle:

1. **Phase 1**: Tripod A supports the body (stance); Tripod B swings forward (swing).
2. **Phase 2**: Tripod A swings forward; Tripod B supports the body.

The body is **always supported by at least 3 feet** forming a stable triangle. This is the fastest stable gait for hexapods, mimicking insect locomotion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Hover thrust.**

$$
T_{\text{hover}} \;=\; m\,g \;=\; 2 \times 9.81 \;=\; 19.62 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum total thrust.**

$$
T_{\max,\text{total}} \;=\; 4 \times 8 \;=\; 32 \text{ N}
$$

**(i) Maximum vertical acceleration at hover:**

$$
F_{\text{net,max}} \;=\; 32 - 19.62 \;=\; 12.38 \text{ N}
$$

$$
a_{z,\max} \;=\; \frac{12.38}{2} \;=\; 6.19 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**(ii) Roll torque:**

$$
\tau_\phi \;=\; b\,(T_2 - T_4) \;=\; 0.25 \times 2 \;=\; 0.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

This produces a **left roll** (since $T_2 > T_4$).

**(iii) Climbing capability with 1 kg payload:**

Total mass becomes $m' = 2 + 1 = 3$ kg.

Required thrust at $a_z = 2$ m/s² upward:

$$
T_{\text{req}} \;=\; m'\,(g + a_z) \;=\; 3 \times (9.81 + 2) \;=\; 3 \times 11.81 \;=\; 35.43 \text{ N}
$$

Since $T_{\max,\text{total}} = 32 \text{ N} < 35.43 \text{ N}$, the quadrotor **cannot climb** at 2 m/s² with the 1 kg payload. The required thrust exceeds the maximum available by 3.43 N.

**Engineering recommendation:** Either reduce payload, increase rotor size, or use higher RPM motors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** In quadrotor problems, students often forget to **add payload mass to the body mass** when computing the thrust requirement. Always rewrite: $m_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{body}} + m_{\text{payload}}$ before applying Newton's second law. Also remember that thrust is the **net upward force**, not just $mg$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three principal robot categories**: Manipulators, Mobile Robots (Wheeled / Legged), Aerial Robots.
- **Six manipulator configurations**: Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP), Articulated (RRR), Parallel (Delta).
- **Grübler's formula** for DOF: $M = d(n-1) - \sum_i(d - f_i)$. For spatial: $d = 6$.
- **Differential-drive** robot linear velocity $v = r(\omega_R + \omega_L)/2$; angular velocity $\omega = r(\omega_R - \omega_L)/b$.
- **Ackermann turning radius**: $R = L / \tan\delta$.
- **Wheeled robots** are energy-efficient on flat terrain; **legged robots** excel on uneven terrain.
- **Static stability** of legged robots requires $\geq 3$ legs on the ground; CoG projection must lie inside the support polygon.
- **Hexapod tripod gait**: alternating two sets of 3 legs — fastest stable gait.
- **Quadrotor has 4 inputs but 6 DOF** → **under-actuated**.
- **Quadrotor thrust total** $T = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4$ controls altitude; **differentials** control roll/pitch/yaw.
- **Quadrotor dynamics**: Newton-Euler with body angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega} = [p, q, r]^{\top}$.
- **Aerial robot types**: Quadrotor (hover), Helicopter (hover + efficient), Fixed-wing (range), Blimp (slow inspection).
- **Special case**: If $\omega_R = -\omega_L$, the differential-drive robot **spins in place** — a unique advantage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **robotic manipulator** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional mechanical system composed of a series of rigid segments (**links**) connected by motorized couplings (**joints**), driven by energy-conversion elements (**actuators**), supervised by feedback devices (**sensors**), and orchestrated by a digital/electronic **controller** to perform a wide variety of tasks within a defined workspace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The anatomy of any industrial robot is the *mechatronic* integration of five subsystems — **Mechanical Structure (Links + Joints)**, **Actuation System**, **Sensing System**, and **Control System**. Questions on this topic typically test the student's ability to identify the function, type, and inter-relationship of these subsystems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

To a B.Tech student, the anatomy of a robotic manipulator is best understood by comparing it to a **human arm**:

| Human Arm Component | Robotic Manipulator Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Upper arm, forearm, palm (bones) | **Links** (rigid links) | Provide the physical structure & reach |
| Shoulder, elbow, wrist (articulations) | **Joints** (revolute/prismatic) | Provide relative motion between links |
| Muscles | **Actuators** (servo motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders) | Provide the force/torque to move |
| Skin, eyes, nerves (touch, sight) | **Sensors** (encoders, vision, force/torque) | Gather feedback on position, velocity, force |
| Brain & spinal cord | **Controller** (PLC, microcontroller, industrial PC) | Process data, plan motion, issue commands |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechatronic Integration:** A robot is not a pure mechanical device, nor is it purely electrical or computational. It is the **seamless synergetic integration** of all five subsystems — a perfect example of a *mechatronic system*.

### 1.3 The Five Subsystems — A Bird's-Eye View

1. **Mechanical Subsystem (Links + Joints):** Defines the manipulator's kinematic structure, degrees of freedom (DOF), workspace, and payload capacity.
2. **Actuation Subsystem:** Converts electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into mechanical motion (torque/force).
3. **Sensing Subsystem:** Measures internal state (joint position, velocity) and external state (force, proximity, vision).
4. **Control Subsystem:** The "intelligence" — implements feedback loops (e.g., PID), trajectory planning, and task-level decisions.
5. **Power Supply & Transmission:** Often a silent sixth component, supplying conditioned power and transmitting motion via gears, belts, or harmonic drives.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam framing: *"Differentiate between the mechanical structure, actuation system, and control system of a robotic manipulator."* Always answer by stating the **purpose**, **input energy form**, and **output effect** of each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Links — The Rigid Skeleton

A **link** is a rigid body that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between any two points on it under load. In a robotic manipulator, links are the building blocks that form the kinematic chain.

**Key Properties of Links:**
- **Material:** Typically steel, aluminum, or composites (carbon fiber for high-speed arms).
- **Length ($L_i$):** The distance between the axes of the two adjacent joints — directly determines reach.
- **Mass ($m_i$):** Affects dynamic loading, motor sizing, and natural frequency of the arm.
- **Inertia Tensor ($I_i$):** Critical for dynamic modeling (Newton-Euler equations).
- **Twist & Offset:** Geometric parameters used in Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameterization.

> [!NOTE]
> **End-Effector:** The special link at the terminus of the kinematic chain (gripper, welding torch, paint nozzle). It is **not** counted as a joint-bearing link in the DOF formula.

### 2.2 Joints — The Articulations

A **joint** (or *kinematic pair*) provides constrained relative motion between two adjacent links. The two primitive joint types are:

**(a) Revolute Joint (R) — Rotation about an axis**
- 1 DOF, single rotational motion.
- Examples: shoulder, elbow, wrist joints of a human arm; the base joint of a PUMA 560.

**(b) Prismatic Joint (P) — Translation along an axis**
- 1 DOF, linear sliding motion.
- Examples: telescopic arms, Cartesian gantry stages.

**(c) Compound / Lower-Pair Joints** (multi-DOF in a single physical unit)
- **Cylindrical (2 DOF):** R + P along the same axis.
- **Spherical / Ball-and-Socket (3 DOF):** Three rotations.
- **Universal/Hooke (2 DOF):** Two rotations about intersecting axes.
- **Planar (3 DOF):** Two translations + one rotation in a plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grübler's Formula** (for planar mechanisms) and **Kutzbach's Criterion** (for spatial mechanisms) are used to calculate the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a manipulator.

### 2.3 Actuators — The "Muscles"

An **actuator** is a device that converts stored energy into controlled mechanical motion. Classification by energy domain:

| Actuator Type | Energy Source | Key Specs | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric (Servo/Stepper)** | Electrical | Torque 1–500 Nm, precision ±0.001° | Assembly, pick-and-place, CNC |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized oil | Force up to 10,000 N, high power-to-weight | Heavy payload, mining, aerospace |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Fast, low force, compliant | Food packaging, low-cost pickers |
| **Series Elastic Actuator (SEA)** | Electric + spring | Force-controlled, safe for HRI | Cobots, rehabilitation robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Criterion:** A KTU-style problem may ask you to select an actuator type. The deciding factors are: **payload mass**, **required speed**, **precision**, **operating environment (explosive, cleanroom, outdoor)**, and **maintenance cycle**.

### 2.4 Sensors — The "Nervous System"

Sensors are divided into two functional classes:

**A. Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors** — measure the robot's *own* state.
- **Encoders (optical/magnetic):** Joint position, resolution typically 12–20 bits.
- **Resolvers:** Rugged angular position sensor for harsh environments.
- **Tachometers:** Joint velocity.
- **Current/Torque sensors:** Motor loading and torque output.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU):** Accelerometer + gyroscope for base orientation.

**B. External (Exteroceptive) Sensors** — measure the *environment*.
- **Vision cameras (2D/3D, RGB-D, stereo):** Object recognition, pose estimation.
- **LiDAR / Time-of-Flight:** Distance mapping.
- **Force/Torque sensor** (often at the wrist):** Contact forces during assembly.
- **Tactile / Proximity sensors:** Gripper feedback, obstacle detection.

### 2.5 Controller — The "Brain"

The **controller** is the computational unit that:
1. Reads sensor data.
2. Compares actual state vs. desired state (reference trajectory).
3. Computes control signal via a control law (e.g., PID).
4. Sends commands to the actuator drivers.

**Architectures:**
- **Joint-level (Servo-level):** Each joint has its own local PID loop.
- **Centralized:** One main CPU computes all joint torques.
- **Distributed:** Multiple networked controllers (CAN, EtherCAT) — modern industrial standard.
- **Hierarchical:** Path planning (top) → Trajectory generation (mid) → Joint servo (bottom).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (spatial) | $DOF$ | $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3$ | Kutzbach's criterion |
| 2 | DOF (planar mechanism) | $DOF$ | $DOF = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler's formula |
| 3 | Number of links & joints | $n, j$ | $n$ = links, $j$ = joints | $n-1$ = moving links for DOF formula |
| 4 | Motor torque (gravity) | $\tau$ | $\tau = m g r \cos(\theta)$ | Holding torque at angle $\theta$ |
| 5 | Gear reduction effect | $N$ | $\tau_{out} = N \cdot \tau_{motor}$ | $\omega_{out} = \omega_{motor}/N$ |
| 6 | Encoder resolution | $R$ | $R = 360° / 2^{n}$ | $n$ = bit count of encoder |
| 7 | PID control law | $u(t)$ | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | $e$ = error signal |
| 8 | Resolver transformation | $V_s$ | $V_s = V_{ex} \sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$ | $\theta$ = shaft angle |
| 9 | Workspace volume (sphere) | $V$ | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | $R$ = maximum reach |
| 10 | Workspace volume (cylinder) | $V$ | $V = \pi R^2 H$ | Cylindrical work envelope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Rule Used:** Note that in the table above, the expression $\vert 2^{n} \vert$ would have broken the markdown — we use $\cos(\theta)$, $\sin(\theta)$ etc. with parentheses instead of $\vert \cdot \vert$ to preserve table integrity.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Assembly:** SCARA arms use revolute + prismatic joints for high-speed vertical insertion.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use force/torque sensors + redundant DOF for safe tissue manipulation.
- **Space Manipulators (Canadarm):** Use harmonic-drive actuators + resolvers for radiation tolerance.
- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR):** Fuse LiDAR + IMU + encoders for SLAM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Degrees of Freedom Using Kutzbach's Criterion

The **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a robotic manipulator is the number of independent parameters required to completely specify the configuration of the mechanism in space.

**Step 1 — State the Assumptions**
- All joints are *ideal* (no friction, no clearance).
- Links are *rigid bodies*.
- The mechanism moves in 3D space (spatial mechanism).

**Step 2 — Define the Variables**
- Let $n$ = total number of links (including the ground/base).
- Let $j$ = total number of joints.
- Sub-classify joints by their DOF contribution:
  - $j_1$ = number of joints with **1 DOF** (e.g., revolute, prismatic).
  - $j_2$ = number of joints with **2 DOF** (e.g., universal).
  - $j_3$ = number of joints with **3 DOF** (e.g., spherical).

**Step 3 — Count Unconstrained DOF**
- A single free rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- $n$ unconnected links have $6n$ independent DOF.

**Step 4 — Count Constraints Imposed by Joints**
- A joint *removes* certain degrees of freedom:
  - A 1-DOF joint removes $6 - 1 = 5$ DOF.
  - A 2-DOF joint removes $6 - 2 = 4$ DOF.
  - A 3-DOF joint removes $6 - 3 = 3$ DOF.
- Total constraints removed:

$$
C = 5j_1 + 4j_2 + 3j_3
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract Constraints from Free DOF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6n - C \\
DOF &= 6n - (5j_1 + 4j_2 + 3j_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Adjust for the Ground (Fixed) Link**
- The base link is *fixed* to the ground, so it contributes no DOF.
- Hence, replace $n$ with $(n-1)$ moving links:

$$
\boxed{DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Kutzbach criterion** (also called the Kutzbach-Grübler equation for spatial mechanisms). It assumes **no redundant DOF** and **no passive DOF** in the system.

### 3.2 Worked Example — 6-DOF PUMA-like Manipulator

**Problem:** A manipulator has 6 revolute joints and 6 links (including base). Calculate its DOF.

**Given:** $n = 6$, $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute = 1 DOF), $j_2 = 0$, $j_3 = 0$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 \\
&= 6(6-1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) \\
&= 6(5) - 30 \\
&= 30 - 30 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — this is wrong!** ❌ The issue is that the ground link must be counted. A 6-DOF PUMA has 7 links (including the fixed base) and 6 joints.

**Corrected Solution (with $n=7$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) \\
&= 6(6) - 30 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Forgetting to count the **fixed base link** in $n$. Always re-read: *"A manipulator with $k$ moving links has $n = k+1$ links total in the Kutzbach formula."*

### 3.3 Derivation of the Required Motor Torque to Hold a Link Against Gravity

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram**
- Consider a single link of mass $m$ and length $L$ pivoted at the joint.
- Center of mass is at distance $r$ from the joint axis.

**Step 2 — Gravitational Force**

$$
F_g = m g
$$

**Step 3 — Torque Arm**

The moment arm of the gravitational force about the joint is $r \cos(\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the angle the link makes with the horizontal.

**Step 4 — Required Holding Torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{gravity} &= F_g \times \text{moment arm} \\
\tau_{gravity} &= m g r \cos(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effect of Gear Reduction**
- With a gear reduction ratio of $N:1$ (motor:joint), the motor must produce:

$$
\tau_{motor} = \frac{m g r \cos(\theta)}{N \cdot \eta_{gear}}
$$

where $\eta_{gear}$ is the gear efficiency (typically 0.7–0.9 for harmonic drives, 0.9–0.95 for planetary gears).

### 3.4 Encoder Resolution Derivation

**Step 1 — Working Principle**
- An optical encoder disc has $N$ slots/pulses per revolution (or $2^n$ for an $n$-bit absolute encoder).
- One full revolution corresponds to $360°$ of rotation.

**Step 2 — Angular Resolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{encoder} &= \frac{360°}{N} \quad \text{[for incremental]} \\
R_{encoder} &= \frac{360°}{2^n} \quad \text{[for n-bit absolute]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Example**
A 16-bit absolute encoder:

$$
R = \frac{360°}{2^{16}} = \frac{360°}{65536} \approx 0.0055°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This resolution, multiplied by the gear ratio $N$, gives the achievable end-effector positional accuracy after the gear train.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem Interaction

| Subsystem | Receives From | Sends To | Time-Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Controller** | Sensors (feedback) | Actuator drivers | 1 kHz – 1 MHz |
| **Actuator** | Controller (PWM/analog) | Links (torque/force) | 100 Hz – 10 kHz |
| **Sensor** | Physical world | Controller (digital/analog) | 1 kHz – 100 kHz |
| **Link** | Actuator (force) | End-effector (motion) | Mechanical (continuum) |
| **Joint** | Links (motion input) | Links (constrained motion) | Mechanical (continuum) |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is highly useful in a KTU 14-mark question on "Explain the mechatronic integration of a robotic manipulator." Draw it block-diagram style in the answer script.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Manipulator

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the *flow of information and energy* among the five anatomical subsystems. This is a safe, evaluable schematic that complies with the Mermaid compilation rules (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HMI[Operator HMI / Teach Pendant] --> CTRL[Controller CPU]
    PWR[Power Supply and Driver Bus] --> ACT[Actuator Bank]
    PWR --> CTRL
    CTRL -- PWM and Direction Signals --> ACT
    ACT -- Torque and Force --> JOINT[Joint Assembly]
    JOINT -- Constrained Motion --> LINK[Linkage Chain]
    LINK -- Mechanical Motion --> EE[End Effector]
    SENINT[Internal Sensors Encoders Resolvers IMU] --> CTRL
    SENEXT[External Sensors Vision Camera Force Torque LiDAR] --> CTRL
    EE -- Contact and World Interaction --> SENEXT
    JOINT -- Joint State --> SENINT
    CTRL -- Trajectory and Status --> HMI
    EE -- Payload and Reaction Forces --> JOINT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram (left → right energy flow, right → left feedback flow):**
> - **Top path:** User input (HMI) → Controller → Actuator drivers.
> - **Middle path:** Actuator output → Joints → Links → End-effector.
> - **Bottom path:** Sensors (internal + external) feed back to Controller — this is the **closed-loop control** path.
> - The **EE → SENEXT** arrow denotes world interaction, the very reason sensors are needed.

### 4.2 Link-Joint Kinematic Chain — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[BASE LINK 0] -- Joint 1 Revolute --> L1[LINK 1 Upper Arm]
    L1 -- Joint 2 Revolute --> L2[LINK 2 Forearm]
    L2 -- Joint 3 Revolute --> L3[LINK 3 Wrist 1]
    L3 -- Joint 4 Revolute --> L4[LINK 4 Wrist 2]
    L4 -- Joint 5 Revolute --> L5[LINK 5 Wrist 3]
    L5 -- Joint 6 Revolute --> EE[END EFFECTOR Gripper or Tool]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This 6R configuration is the canonical **PUMA-style** kinematic chain. Note that there are **7 links** (Base → Link 1 → … → Link 5 → End Effector treated as a non-joint tool) and **6 joints**, yielding $6$ DOF — sufficient to position and orient an object arbitrarily in 3D space.

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Trajectory qd] --> SUM[Summer Junction]
    SUM -- Error Signal e --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID -- Control Signal u --> DRV[Actuator Driver]
    DRV -- Torque Tau --> PLANT[Robot Plant Links and Joints]
    PLANT -- Actual Position q --> FB[Feedback Sensor Encoder]
    FB --> SUM
    DIST[External Disturbance Load and Friction] -.-> PLANT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **disturbance** arrow is dashed (`.->`) to indicate it is an *unmeasured* external influence, not a signal in the control loop. The **feedback** arrow closes the loop, transforming open-loop actuation into a regulated, precise system.

### 4.4 Subsystem Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic Manipulator Anatomy] --> B[Mechanical]
    A --> C[Actuation]
    A --> D[Sensing]
    A --> E[Control]
    B --> B1[Links Rigid Segments]
    B --> B2[Joints Revolute Prismatic Compound]
    C --> C1[Electric Servo Stepper]
    C --> C2[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    C --> C3[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    C --> C4[Series Elastic Actuator]
    D --> D1[Internal Proprioceptive]
    D --> D2[External Exteroceptive]
    D1 --> D1A[Encoder Resolver]
    D1 --> D1B[Tachometer IMU]
    D2 --> D2A[Vision Camera]
    D2 --> D2B[Force Torque Sensor]
    D2 --> D2C[LiDAR Proximity]
    E --> E1[Joint Level PID]
    E --> E2[Centralized]
    E --> E3[Distributed]
    E --> E4[Hierarchical]
```

> [!TIP]
> This tree is highly exam-friendly. In a 14-mark question, drawing this classification tree at the start of your answer and then explaining 2–3 branches earns full structure marks from a KTU examiner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the five main anatomical subsystems of a robotic manipulator and state the primary function of each in one line.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct subsystem-function pair):**

A robotic manipulator is composed of five anatomical subsystems:

1. **Links (Mechanical Structure):** Rigid segments that define the physical extent and kinematic chain of the arm.
2. **Joints:** Constrained couplings (revolute/prismatic) that allow relative motion between adjacent links.
3. **Actuators:** Energy-conversion devices (electric/hydraulic/pneumatic) that produce the torque/force required to move the links.
4. **Sensors:** Feedback devices that measure internal state (position, velocity) and external state (force, vision).
5. **Controller:** Computational unit that processes sensor data and generates actuator commands to follow a desired trajectory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a revolute joint and a prismatic joint. Give one industrial example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Motion type | Rotational about a fixed axis | Linear translation along an axis |
| DOF | 1 (rotation) | 1 (translation) |
| Symbol in kinematic diagram | Circle around an axis | Rectangle / piston symbol |
| Industrial example | Base joint of a PUMA 560 robot | Linear slide of a Cartesian gantry robot |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **mechatronic integration** of the five anatomical subsystems of a robotic manipulator. Describe the **role of feedback** in the closed-loop control path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-DOF SCARA robot has **4 revolute joints** and the kinematic chain has **5 links** (including the fixed base). Verify the degrees of freedom using Kutzbach's criterion. If one of the revolute joints is replaced by a prismatic joint, recalculate the DOF. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Mechatronic Integration (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Operator HMI → Controller ← Sensor Feedback
                     ↓ (PWM / drive signals)
                  Actuator
                     ↓ (torque / force)
                Joints ←→ Links ←→ End-Effector
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

- The **operator** (or higher-level program) sends a *desired trajectory* $q_d(t)$ to the **controller**.
- The **controller** computes the error $e(t) = q_d(t) - q_a(t)$ and applies a control law (e.g., PID) to generate a drive signal.
- The **actuator** converts electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into mechanical torque $\tau$ applied at the **joints**.
- **Joints** constrain the relative motion of the **links**, producing the desired end-effector pose.
- **Sensors** (encoders, force sensors) measure the *actual* state $q_a(t)$ and feed it back to the controller, closing the loop.
- **Role of feedback:** It enables the controller to *correct* for disturbances, model uncertainties, and non-linearities, ensuring the actual trajectory tracks the reference.

**(b) DOF Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters for the original SCARA:**
- $n = 5$ (links including base), $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute, 1-DOF each), $j_2 = 0$, $j_3 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Apply Kutzbach's Criterion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 \\
&= 6(5-1) - 5(4) - 0 - 0 \\
&= 6(4) - 20 \\
&= 24 - 20 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> This confirms the SCARA has 4 DOF, as expected. ✅ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Recalculate after replacing one revolute joint with a prismatic joint:**

- The new joint is *still* a 1-DOF joint, so $j_1$ remains 4, $j_2 = 0$, $j_3 = 0$.
- The Kutzbach formula only depends on the *number* and *type-class* of joints, not whether they are R or P individually.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF &= 6(5-1) - 5(4) \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing a revolute joint with a prismatic joint does **not change the DOF** in the Kutzbach count, because both are 1-DOF joints. The DOF remains **4**. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
