> - Students often confuse the *DOF of a joint* with the *type* of joint. Both R and P are 1-DOF — they remove 5 DOF each in spatial Kutzbach.
> - Students frequently forget to count the **fixed base** as a link, leading to $n=4$ instead of $n=5$. This drops your answer to $DOF = -2$, which is mathematically impossible and immediately signals an error.
> - Always state the formula *before* substituting — this earns a "method" mark even if the arithmetic is wrong.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify **actuators** used in robotic manipulators based on the energy domain. Compare electric servo motors with hydraulic actuators, listing at least three merits and three demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic manipulator uses a **harmonic-drive gear** with a reduction ratio of $N = 100:1$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. The end-effector carries a payload of $m = 5$ kg, and the relevant link has its center of mass at $r = 0.3$ m from the joint axis. The link itself weighs $m_L = 8$ kg. The link is held stationary at an angle $\theta = 60°$ from the horizontal. Calculate the **holding torque required at the motor shaft**. (Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².) **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Actuator Classification (7 marks)**

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Class | Energy Source | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Electric | Electrical | DC servo, AC servo, stepper, BLDC |
| Hydraulic | Pressurized oil | Linear cylinder, rotary vane |
| Pneumatic | Compressed air | Linear cylinder, rotary vane |
| New-generation | Electrical + elastic element | Series Elastic Actuator (SEA) |

**Comparison (4 marks — 2 merits + 2 demerits for each):**

| Property | Electric Servo Motor | Hydraulic Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| **Merit 1** | High precision (±0.001°) and repeatability | Very high power-to-weight ratio |
| **Merit 2** | Easy computer interfacing (PWM, CAN) | High force/torque, suitable for heavy payloads |
| **Merit 3** | Low maintenance, no fluid leaks | Smooth, inherently compliant motion |
| **Demerit 1** | Limited torque density (overheats under stall) | Requires pump, reservoir, valves — bulky |
| **Demerit 2** | Heat dissipation issues at high load | Risk of oil leakage, fire hazard |
| **Demerit 3** | Gearing backlash in harmonic drives | Slow response due to fluid compressibility |

**(b) Holding Torque Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the loads:**
- Payload: $m = 5$ kg
- Link mass: $m_L = 8$ kg
- Total mass creating torque: $m_{total} = m + m_L = 13$ kg
- Moment arm: $r = 0.3$ m
- Angle: $\theta = 60°$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2 — Compute the total gravitational force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m_{total} \cdot g \\
&= 13 \times 9.81 \\
&= 127.53 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the torque at the joint (output side of gearbox):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{joint} &= F_g \cdot r \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
&= 127.53 \times 0.3 \times \cos(60°) \\
&= 127.53 \times 0.3 \times 0.5 \\
&= 19.13 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to motor shaft torque using gear ratio and efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{motor} &= \frac{\tau_{joint}}{N \cdot \eta} \\
&= \frac{19.13}{100 \times 0.85} \\
&= \frac{19.13}{85} \\
&\approx 0.225 \text{ Nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The motor must produce a holding torque of approximately **$\tau_{motor} \approx 0.225$ Nm** at its shaft.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> - Many students forget the **efficiency factor** $\eta$. Without it, the answer becomes $0.191$ Nm, which is physically unrealistic for a friction-laden gear train. Always include $\eta$ for a harmonic drive ($\eta \approx 0.7$–$0.85$).
> - Some students use $\sin(\theta)$ instead of $\cos(\theta)$ — the convention here is $\theta$ measured from the **horizontal**, so the moment arm of the vertical gravitational force is $r \cos(\theta)$.
> - Forgetting to **add the link's own mass** to the payload is another common error, leading to a 30–40% under-estimation of the required torque.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator**

- [x] **Five Subsystems:** Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller. Memorize their functions.
- [x] **Kutzbach's Criterion:** $DOF = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3$. **Always include the base link in $n$.**
- [x] **Two Primitive Joints:** Revolute (R, rotational) and Prismatic (P, linear). Both are 1-DOF.
- [x] **Compound Joints:** Universal (2 DOF), Spherical (3 DOF). Rare in industrial arms, common in wrists.
- [x] **Actuator Energy Domains:** Electric (most common), Hydraulic (heavy), Pneumatic (light/fast), SEA (safe).
- [x] **Sensors — Two Classes:** Proprioceptive (internal: encoders, IMU) and Exteroceptive (external: vision, force).
- [x] **PID Control Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ — the workhorse of robot joint control.
- [x] **Gear Train Effect:** Output torque scales by $N \cdot \eta$, output speed scales by $1/N$.
- [x] **Encoder Resolution:** $R = 360° / 2^n$ for an $n$-bit absolute encoder.
- [x] **Holding Torque Formula:** $\tau = m g r \cos(\theta)$ — divides by $N \cdot \eta$ at the motor shaft.
- [x] **Closed-Loop is Mandatory:** Open-loop control is rarely used in manipulators due to disturbances.
- [x] **Mechatronics Mindset:** Every anatomical subsystem is *coupled* — never treat them in isolation.
- [x] **PUMA-style chain:** 6R, 7 links (with base) = 6 DOF — the canonical example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks you to *"explain the anatomy of a robotic manipulator,"* structure your answer as:
> 1. **Block diagram (3 marks)**
> 2. **One-paragraph description of each of the 5 subsystems (5 marks)**
> 3. **Mechatronic integration & feedback explanation (3 marks)**
> 4. **One numerical example — DOF, torque, or encoder resolution (3 marks)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Kinematic vs Closed Kinematic Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Kinematic Chains

A **Kinematic Chain** is an assembly of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints (kinematic pairs) that permits relative motion between the connected links. The chain defines the geometric and mechanical pathway through which motion, force, and velocity are transmitted from a fixed base to an end-effector (tool/gripper).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A manipulator is classified as **Open Chain** or **Closed Chain** based on the topological connectivity of its links — not on the number of joints, payload capacity, or workspace volume.

### 1.1.1 Open Kinematic Chain

An **Open Kinematic Chain** is a serial linkage in which the links are connected end-to-end in a single, un-branched sequence. **Only one link is anchored to the ground (base)**, while the **terminal link (end-effector) remains free in space**. There exists **no closed loop** anywhere in the structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** Traverse the chain starting from the base → every link is visited exactly once before reaching the end-effector. If you can never return to a previously visited link, the chain is open.

### 1.1.2 Closed Kinematic Chain

A **Closed Kinematic Chain** is a linkage in which **at least one kinematic loop** exists. This means that starting from any link and traversing the joints, one can return to the starting link via a different path. In a fully closed chain, **the end-effector is constrained between two or more ground-anchored links**, or multiple serial branches meet at a common floating link.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifier:** If, while tracing links from base to end-effector, you can return to the base without reversing direction through any joint, the structure is closed.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Instantly Clear 🎯

### 🍴 The Fork Analogy (Open Chain)

Imagine a **single fork** held in your hand. The hand acts as the **ground (base)**, and the tips of the fork are the **end-effector**. Every joint in the chain (your wrist, knuckles, finger joints) connects serially. There is **no second path** back to the hand. The structure is *linear and unconstrained* at its tip.

- A **robot arm** such as the **PUMA 560** or the **SCARA** behaves identically: the base is bolted to the floor, the joints are arranged in a series, and the gripper floats freely at the tip.

### 🔗 The Bicycle Frame Analogy (Closed Chain)

Now imagine a **bicycle frame** — the front wheel, handlebar, and rear wheel are all connected to the **same rigid triangular base frame (ground)** through multiple struts. The pedal mechanism forms a **four-bar closed loop** within the larger structure. The motion of one strut is mechanically coupled to every other strut in the loop.

- A **Stewart Platform** used in flight simulators, or a **Delta Robot** used in packaging lines, exhibits this exact same behaviour: the moving platform is suspended by multiple parallel legs, all anchored to a common base ring.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:**
> - **OPEN** = *One* path only, *P*urely serial → like the word "**S-E-R-I-A-L**"
> - **CLOSED** = *Loop* present, *P*arallel branches meet → like a *treasure-map loop* that returns home 🗺️

---

## 1.3 Quick Visual Cue

| Feature | Open Chain | Closed Chain |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Tree (no cycles) | Graph with ≥ 1 cycle |
| Base attachment | One end only | Both/multiple ends |
| Workspace shape | Large, irregular | Compact, symmetric |
| Rigidity | Lower | Higher |
| Manipulability | High | Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Throughout KTU examinations, when a manipulator is described as a **"6-DOF serial robot"**, it is implicitly an **open chain** unless a parallel branch or closed loop is explicitly mentioned. **Do not assume otherwise.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Taxonomy of Kinematic Chains

A kinematic chain can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Topology** → Open vs. Closed (the focus of this module)
2. **Joint Type Mix** → Revolute-only (RR), Prismatic-only (PP), or Mixed (RP)
3. **Dimensionality** → Planar (2-D motion) vs. Spatial (3-D motion)

The first axis — **topology** — is what governs the **mathematical structure of the forward and inverse kinematics equations**, the **stiffness matrix**, and the **Jacobian rank profile**.

---

## 2.2 The Grübler–Kutzbach Mobility Formula (Master Equation)

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — also called the **mobility (M)** — of a planar or spatial mechanism is the most important scalar quantity in linkage analysis. The KTU board frequently asks students to compute it directly.

### 2.2.1 Spatial (3-D) Mechanisms

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

Where:

- $n$ = total number of **links** (including the ground)
- $j_i$ = number of joints having **$i$ degrees of freedom** ($i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$)
- The coefficient **6** corresponds to the **6 rigid-body DOFs** in 3-D space (3 translations + 3 rotations)

### 2.2.2 Planar (2-D) Mechanisms

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

For strictly planar motion, the 3 rigid-body DOFs are: 2 translations + 1 rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall:** In a **closed chain**, the formula correctly accounts for the loop constraints *implicitly* through the link count $n$. **Do not subtract loop equations separately** — that would double-count constraints.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description / Use-Case | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6-i)j_i$ | Grübler's spatial mobility | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Grübler's planar mobility | dimensionless |
| 3 | $M_{open} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{joints}} f_i$ | Sum of joint freedoms (open chain shortcut) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\vec{p}_{ee} = f(\vec{q})$ | Forward kinematics (open chain) | meters |
| 5 | $\vec{J}(\vec{q})\,\dot{\vec{q}} = \vec{v}_{ee}$ | Jacobian velocity mapping | m/s, rad/s |
| 6 | $\det\bigl(\vec{J}(\vec{q})\bigr) = 0$ | **Singularity** condition (loss of DOF) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_{closed} > K_{open}$ | Closed-chain stiffness is **higher** (qualitative) | N/m |
| 8 | $W_{closed} \subset W_{open}$ | Closed-chain workspace is a **subset** | m³ |
| 9 | $P_{open,IK} = 8$ | PUMA-type IK solutions (up to 8) | real solutions |
| 10 | $P_{closed,IK} = 1$ | Closed chain IK has a **unique** solution in workspace | real solutions |

> **Notation:** $\vec{p}_{ee}$ — end-effector position vector; $\vec{q}$ — joint variable vector; $\vec{J}$ — Jacobian matrix; $\vec{v}_{ee}$ — end-effector linear/angular velocity vector.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Pros / Cons Matrix (High-Yield for Theory)

### 2.4.1 Open Kinematic Chain

- ✅ **Large, dexterous workspace** — the end-effector can reach around obstacles and into cavities.
- ✅ **Simple forward kinematics** — direct recursive DH transformation, $A_1 A_2 A_3 \dots A_n$.
- ✅ **Independent joint control** — each actuator is decoupled.
- ❌ **Low stiffness** — errors and deflections **accumulate** link by link (worst-case stack-up).
- ❌ **Lower payload-to-weight ratio** — actuators must support all downstream link masses.
- ❌ **Multiple IK solutions** — kinematic ambiguity complicates path planning.

### 2.4.2 Closed Kinematic Chain

- ✅ **High structural stiffness** — external loads are reacted by **multiple struts simultaneously** (load-sharing).
- ✅ **High accuracy & repeatability** — closed-loop geometry constrains errors.
- ✅ **High payload-to-weight ratio** — motors are often mounted on the **base**, not the moving links.
- ❌ **Smaller, more constrained workspace** — singular surfaces lie *inside* the reachable set.
- ❌ **Coupled joint motion** — actuation of one leg requires coordinated motion of others.
- ❌ **Complex forward kinematics** — requires solving **constraint equations** (e.g., distance-preservation loops).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Open chain** dominates in **welding, painting, pick-and-place** (large workspace, moderate accuracy).
> - **Closed chain** dominates in **CNC machine tool hexapods, flight simulators, surgical robots, and high-speed pick-and-place delta robots** (high stiffness, low inertia).

---

## 2.5 Hybrid Configurations (Examiner Favourite!)

KTU examiners often ask: *"Is the SCARA robot open or closed?"* The answer depends on **sub-assembly analysis**.

- **SCARA Arm:** The **primary 3-revolute arm** is **open**. (The optional vertical prismatic joint extends the chain — still open.)
- **Delta Robot:** The **moving platform is closed** (three parallel RRR legs converge onto a common plate), but each leg is internally **open**.
- **Stewart Platform:** **Purely closed** (6 UPS legs connecting base to platform).
- **Knuckle-Boom Crane:** **Open chain** (serial hydraulic actuators), but the **load sheave block** can introduce a *passive* closed loop with the cable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** If the manipulator contains a **parallel sub-mechanism** (e.g., a four-bar linkage within a larger serial arm), classify the **overall topology** by the **primary serial path**, then note the **embedded closed sub-chain** explicitly. Examiners deduct marks if students ignore sub-loops.

---

## 2.6 Mathematical Structure of the Constraint Equations

For a **closed chain** with $k$ independent loops, the loop-closure vector equation is:

$$\vec{f}_1(\vec{q}_1) - \vec{f}_2(\vec{q}_2) = \vec{0}$$

Where $\vec{f}_i$ are the position vectors tracing the two distinct paths of the $i^{th}$ loop. This **scalar constraint** reduces the number of independent joint variables from $\sum j_i$ to $\sum j_i - k$, which is exactly what the Grübler formula captures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Grübler's Mobility Formula for a Spatial Linkage

We start from the **unconstrained** DOF count: each of the $n$ links in 3-D space has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations), giving $6n$ total. The single **ground link** is fixed, so we subtract 6 to obtain the mobile DOFs:

$$M_{free} = 6n - 6 = 6(n-1)$$

Now we subtract the **constraints imposed by each joint**. A joint with $i$ DOF removes $(6 - i)$ constraints:

$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6 - i) \cdot j_i$$

Expanding the summation:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1j_5$$

This is the **complete Grübler–Kutzbach** formula. ✅

> **Assumption:** The joints must be **simple** (single-DOF primitives such as R, P, H, C, S) for the coefficients to be valid. A **compound joint** (e.g., a universal joint modelled as two intersecting revolutes) must be decomposed first.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: 3R Open Chain (Planar)

**Problem:** A planar robot has 4 links and 3 revolute joints. Compute mobility.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$n = 4$, $j_1 = 3$ (all revolute, 1-DOF each)

**Step 2 — Apply the planar Grübler equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
&= 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 0 \\
&= 3(3) - 6 \\
&= 9 - 6 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:** The end-effector has **3 planar DOFs** — namely, $x$, $y$, and $\phi$ (orientation). This matches the intuitive count of 3 independent joint angles. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: 4-Bar Closed Chain (Planar)

**Problem:** A planar four-bar linkage has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Compute mobility.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
$n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$

**Step 2 — Apply the planar Grübler equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 \\
&= 3(4-1) - 2(4) \\
&= 9 - 8 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:** The mechanism has **1 DOF** — typically the input crank angle. This is the classic result for a four-bar linkage used in **vehicle suspensions, sewing machines, and folding mechanisms**. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Comparison of Open vs. Closed 3-DOF Spatial Chains

**Problem:** Compare mobility of (a) a 3R spatial open chain and (b) a 3-RSS symmetric closed chain (simple Delta-like leg).

### Case (a): 3R Open Chain

Variables: $n = 4$ (base + 3 links), $j_1 = 3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 \\
&= 6(4-1) - 5(3) \\
&= 18 - 15 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** 3 spatial DOFs. ✅

### Case (b): Delta-Like Symmetric Closed Chain

For a simplified 3-leg Delta with 1 spherical joint per leg and 3 revolute actuators on the base:

- $n = 8$ (1 base + 3 upper arms + 3 forearms + 1 platform)
- $j_1 = 3$ (actuated revolutes) + $j_3 = 3$ (spherical joints, 3-DOF each) = 3 + 3 = 6 joints total

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(8-1) - 5(3) - 3(3) \\
&= 42 - 15 - 9 \\
&= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction Note:** The naive Grübler count overestimates mobility because **3 geometric loop equations** must be imposed (one per leg). Each loop closure removes 6 DOF in 3-D space (the 6-DOF pose of the platform). Effective mobility:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{effective} &= 18 - 3 \times 6 \\
&= 18 - 18 \\
&\rightarrow \text{structure} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

When the **3 actuators** are added as constrained freedoms, the platform achieves the expected **3 translational DOFs**. The full derivation requires the **Kutzbach corrected form** with **redundant constraints**, but the key insight is that the **closed chain's loop equations convert joint variables into a much smaller set of independent inputs**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 2: Why Closed Chains Are Stiffer — The Load-Sharing Argument

Consider a single actuator supporting a transverse load $F$ at the end-effector.

**Open chain stiffness:**
$$k_{open} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k_i} \right)^{-1}$$

The total compliance is the **sum** of individual joint compliances (springs-in-series). The stiffness is dominated by the **weakest joint**.

**Closed chain stiffness (with $m$ parallel struts):**
$$k_{closed} \approx m \cdot k_{strut}$$

The struts act as **springs in parallel**, so stiffnesses **add**. For $m = 6$ identical struts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{closed} &\approx 6 \cdot k_{strut} \\
k_{open} &\approx \frac{k_{link}}{n} \quad \text{(serial dominance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$\frac{k_{closed}}{k_{open}} \approx \frac{6 \cdot n \cdot k_{strut}}{k_{link}} \gg 1$$

> This is the **fundamental engineering reason** why CNC machine tools and surgical robots use closed kinematic architectures. ✅

---

## 3.6 Derivation 3: Workspace Volume Comparison (Qualitative)

For a planar $n$R open chain with link length $L$:

$$W_{open} \approx \pi (nL)^2$$

For a planar closed chain with $m$ struts of length $L$ forming a polygon:

$$W_{closed} \approx \pi \left( \frac{mL}{2\sin(\pi/m)} \right)^2 \cdot \eta$$

Where $\eta < 1$ is the **workspace reduction factor** due to collision-avoidance between struts and to the inscribed geometry.

For $m = 3$ (Delta-like planar):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{closed} &\approx \pi \left( \frac{3L}{2\sin(60°)} \right)^2 \cdot \eta \\
&= \pi \left( \frac{3L}{\sqrt{3}} \right)^2 \cdot \eta \\
&= 3\pi L^2 \cdot \eta
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with a 3R open chain of total reach $3L$:

$$\frac{W_{open}}{W_{closed}} \approx \frac{9\pi L^2}{3\pi L^2 \eta} = \frac{3}{\eta} > 1$$

So the **open chain has strictly larger workspace** for equal link length. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Topological Comparison (Mermaid Graph)

The following Mermaid block renders the **structural connectivity** of both chain types. Open chains are trees (acyclic); closed chains contain loops.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN KINEMATIC CHAIN - Serial Tree"]
        B1[Base GROUND] --> J1[Revolute J1]
        J1 --> L1[Link 1]
        L1 --> J2[Revolute J2]
        J2 --> L2[Link 2]
        L2 --> J3[Revolute J3]
        J3 --> L3[Link 3]
        L3 --> J4[Revolute J4]
        J4 --> L4[Link 4]
        L4 --> J5[Revolute J5]
        J5 --> L5[Link 5]
        L5 --> J6[Revolute J6]
        J6 --> EE1[End Effector FREE]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED KINEMATIC CHAIN - With Loop"]
        B2[Base GROUND] --> JA1[Revolute A1]
        B2 --> JB1[Revolute B1]
        B2 --> JC1[Revolute C1]
        JA1 --> UA1[Upper Arm 1]
        JB1 --> UB1[Upper Arm 2]
        JC1 --> UC1[Upper Arm 3]
        UA1 --> SP1[Spherical Joint 1]
        UB1 --> SP2[Spherical Joint 2]
        UC1 --> SP3[Spherical Joint 3]
        SP1 --> PLAT[Moving Platform]
        SP2 --> PLAT
        SP3 --> PLAT
        PLAT --> EE2[End Effector ON PLATFORM]
    end

    style B1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#7e2f2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EE1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EE2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PLAT fill:#c08b00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SP1 fill:#c08b00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SP2 fill:#c08b00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SP3 fill:#c08b00,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The OPEN chain has **no cycle** — start at the base, walk to the end-effector, and you stop. The CLOSED chain has **three distinct paths** from the base ring to the moving platform, and those paths form **three independent loops** that share no common intermediate link.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram shows the **signal/force flow architecture** of both chain types, illustrating why control and stiffness differ.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OPEN_ARCH["OPEN CHAIN - CONTROL FLOW"]
        C1[Controller] --> ACT1[Joint 1 Actuator]
        ACT1 --> LNK1[Link 1]
        LNK1 --> ACT2[Joint 2 Actuator]
        ACT2 --> LNK2[Link 2]
        LNK2 --> ACT3[Joint 3 Actuator]
        ACT3 --> LNK3[Link 3]
        LNK3 --> EEO[End Effector]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED_ARCH["CLOSED CHAIN - PARALLEL FORCE FLOW"]
        CC[Central Controller] --> MA1[Actuator 1]
        CC --> MA2[Actuator 2]
        CC --> MA3[Actuator 3]
        MA1 --> ST1[Strut 1]
        MA2 --> ST2[Strut 2]
        MA3 --> ST3[Strut 3]
        ST1 --> PLT[Common Platform]
        ST2 --> PLT
        ST3 --> PLT
        PLT --> EEC[End Effector]
        PLT -.CONSTRAINT LOOP.-> ST1
        PLT -.CONSTRAINT LOOP.-> ST2
        PLT -.CONSTRAINT LOOP.-> ST3
    end

    style C1 fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CC fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EEO fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EEC fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PLT fill:#c08b00,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** In the **open chain**, errors propagate **sequentially** (cascaded). In the **closed chain**, errors are **averaged and constrained** by the simultaneous geometric feedback of multiple struts. This is why a Delta robot with three identical struts achieves $\pm 0.05$ mm repeatability while a 6R arm of comparable reach achieves only $\pm 0.1$ mm.

---

## 4.3 Workspace Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph WS["WORKSPACE COMPARISON PLAN VIEW"]
        OWS[Open Chain Workspace IRREGULAR BULBOUS REGION]
        CWS[Closed Chain Workspace COMPACT OVAL REGION]
    end

    style OWS fill:#90caf9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CWS fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Engineering Note:** The open chain's reachable volume is **topologically homeomorphic to a thick spherical shell** (sphere with an inner dead zone), while the closed chain's reachable volume is **topologically homeomorphic to a thickened disk** (always with internal singularities).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** Define an **Open Kinematic Chain** with a suitable example. State **two advantages** of the open chain over the closed chain.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An open kinematic chain is a serial linkage in which links are connected end-to-end via joints, with **only one end fixed to the ground** and the **end-effector free in space**. There is no closed loop in the structure.

**Example:** The 6-DOF **PUMA 560** industrial robot arm, where six revolute joints are connected in series between the base and the gripper.

**Advantages over closed chain (any 2):**
1. **Larger and more dexterous workspace** — the end-effector can be positioned in a greater volume for the same link length.
2. **Simpler forward kinematics** — the homogeneous transform is a direct product $T = A_1 A_2 A_3 \ldots A_n$, with no loop-closure equations to solve.
3. **Simpler independent joint control** — each actuator can be commanded without coordinating the others.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for two advantages)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** What is a **Closed Kinematic Chain**? Give **one industrial example** and explain why it is preferred for high-precision applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A closed kinematic chain contains **at least one kinematic loop**, in which the end-effector (or a moving platform) is constrained between two or more branches that are anchored to a common base.

**Industrial Example:** The **Stewart Platform** (hexapod) used in flight simulators, or the **Delta Robot** used in high-speed pick-and-place packaging lines.

**Reason for high-precision preference:** In a closed chain, external loads and disturbances are **shared simultaneously by multiple parallel struts**, dramatically increasing the **effective structural stiffness**. Furthermore, the **loop-closure geometric constraints** mechanically correct positional errors that would otherwise accumulate in a serial chain, leading to **higher repeatability (typically ±0.01–0.05 mm)**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for stiffness/repeatability reason)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **general structure** of an open kinematic chain manipulator. List **five key differences** between open and closed kinematic chains.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For a spatial robot with **$n = 6$ links** and **6 revolute joints**, compute the **degrees of freedom** using Grübler's formula. Verify your result by the **joint-freedom-sum method** for an open chain.

### ✅ Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Structure & Differences

**Structure of an Open Chain Manipulator:**

An open-chain manipulator consists of the following elements connected in series:

1. **Base link** — bolted to the ground (the only anchored link).
2. **Links** ($L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n$) — rigid bodies carrying the joints and motors.
3. **Joints** ($J_1, J_2, \dots, J_n$) — typically revolute (R) or prismatic (P).
4. **End-effector** — gripper, welding torch, or tool mounted on the **terminal link**.

The transformation from the base frame to the end-effector frame is:

$$T_0^n = A_1(q_1) \cdot A_2(q_2) \cdot A_3(q_3) \cdot A_4(q_4) \cdot A_5(q_5) \cdot A_6(q_6)$$

where each $A_i$ is a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix expressed via Denavit–Hartenberg parameters.

**Five Key Differences:**

| # | Aspect | Open Chain | Closed Chain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Topology | Serial, acyclic | Contains at least one loop |
| 2 | Stiffness | Lower (errors accumulate) | Higher (load shared) |
| 3 | Workspace | Larger, irregular | Smaller, symmetric |
| 4 | Payload | Lower (motors carry downstream mass) | Higher (motors on base) |
| 5 | IK solutions | Multiple (e.g., 8 for PUMA) | Unique inside workspace |

*(3 marks for sketch & structure description, 4 marks for 5 differences)*

#### Part (b) — DOF Computation

**Given:** $n = 6$ links (including ground), $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints (1-DOF each).

**Method 1 — Grübler's spatial formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
&= 6(6-1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
&= 30 - 30 \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — this gives 0, which is wrong!** The issue is that in a **typical 6R industrial arm**, the **base is the ground link**, and we have **6 moving links + 1 ground = 7 total links**, not 6. Let us correct the count:

**Corrected count:** $n = 7$ (1 ground + 6 moving links), $j_1 = 6$ (revolute joints).

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $M = 6$ DOF — exactly the **3 translations + 3 rotations** required to position and orient a rigid body in 3-D space. ✅

**Method 2 — Joint-freedom-sum (open chain shortcut):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{open} &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** Both methods agree → $M = 6$ DOF. ✅

*(4 marks for Grübler computation, 2 marks for verification, 1 mark for physical interpretation)*

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a sketch the **working principle of a closed kinematic chain** manipulator. Discuss **why closed chains are preferred for high-speed pick-and-place applications** (cite **stiffness, inertia, and workspace** considerations).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A planar mechanism has **6 links and 7 revolute joints**. Determine the **number of independent loops** and the **degrees of freedom**. Comment on whether the structure is a **mechanism, structure, or pre-loaded structure**.

### ✅ Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Closed-Chain Working Principle

**Working Principle (using Delta Robot as reference):**

In a closed-chain manipulator such as the **Delta Robot**:

- A **stationary upper base** holds three (or more) **actuated revolute joints** at $120°$ intervals.
- Each actuator drives an **upper arm** that connects, via a **parallelogram four-bar sub-chain**, to a **forearm**.
- The three forearms converge onto a **moving triangular platform** (the end-effector) through **ball or spherical joints**.
- When the three actuators rotate, the parallelogram sub-chains constrain the platform to **pure translational motion** (3 DOF: $x$, $y$, $z$), with **no rotation**.
- The **loop-closure equations** of the three parallelograms enforce **constant orientation** of the platform.

**Why preferred for high-speed pick-and-place:**

1. **Low moving inertia:** The motors are mounted on the **fixed base**; only the **lightweight forearms and platform** move. The mass ratio (payload : moving mass) is very high, enabling accelerations of **20–50 g** (typical of packaging-line delta robots).
2. **High stiffness:** External forces on the platform are reacted by **three or more struts in parallel**, giving stiffness $\approx 3k$ to $6k$ of a single strut. This minimizes **deflection under high acceleration**.
3. **Compact workspace optimization:** Although the absolute workspace is smaller, the **workspace volume per unit of reach** is extremely high, and the workspace is **symmetric and predictable** — ideal for vision-guided picking.
4. **No singularity within the primary workspace:** The Delta's central singularity is at the **base center**, which is unreachable — so the operational workspace is **singularity-free**.

*(3 marks for sketch and working principle, 4 marks for justification of high-speed use)*

#### Part (b) — Planar Mechanism Analysis

**Given:** $n = 6$ links, $j_1 = 7$ revolute joints (planar, so all 1-DOF).

**Step 1 — Apply the planar Grübler formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 \\
&= 3(6-1) - 2(7) \\
&= 15 - 14 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of independent loops ($L$):**

For a planar single-DOF mechanism, the number of independent loops is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= j_1 - n + 1 \\
&= 7 - 6 + 1 \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So the mechanism has **2 independent kinematic loops**. ✅

**Step 3 — Classify the structure:**

Since $M = 1 > 0$, the structure is a **mechanism** (not a structure, which would have $M = 0$; not a pre-loaded structure, which would have $M < 0$).

- **Structure** ($M = 0$): a statically determinate truss or frame — no motion possible.
- **Pre-loaded structure** ($M < 0$): redundant constraints with no mobility — statically indeterminate.
- **Mechanism** ($M > 0$): controllable motion exists — our case with $M = 1$.

**Physical interpretation:** A single-input crank or slider can drive the entire 6-link, 2-loop mechanism through one degree of freedom, with all other joint motions uniquely determined by the loop-closure constraints.

*(3 marks for Grübler computation, 2 marks for loop count, 2 marks for classification and physical interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Link counting error (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often **forget to include the ground link** in $n$, or **double-count the end-effector**. Always list every rigid body: $n$ = ground + all moving links.
>
> 2. **Confusing joints and links (–1 mark):** In a 6R arm, students sometimes write $n = 6$ and $j_1 = 6$, leading to $M = 0$ (a structure). The correct count is $n = 7$ (ground + 6 moving links) → $M = 6$.
>
> 3. **Applying 3-D formula to a planar problem (–2 marks):** Use $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1$ for 2-D and $M = 6(n-1) - \sum (6-i)j_i$ for 3-D. Mixing them is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the loop count for closed chains (–1 mark):** Even if Grübler gives a non-integer or zero result, **state the loop count explicitly** and interpret the physical meaning.
>
> 5. **Writing $|x|$ with vertical pipes in tables (–0 marks but formatting penalty):** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(x)$ in LaTeX to keep markdown tables intact.
>
> 6. **Vague "open/closed" answer without topology justification (–1 mark):** Always state *why* — mention "no kinematic loop" or "at least one closed loop" explicitly.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **The "Must-Know" list before walking into the KTU exam hall:**

- ⭐ An **open kinematic chain** has links in **series** with **no kinematic loop**; **one end fixed**, the other **free**.
- ⭐ A **closed kinematic chain** has **at least one loop**; the end-effector is **constrained between multiple ground-anchored branches**.
- ⭐ **Grübler's spatial formula:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$
- ⭐ **Grübler's planar formula:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$
- ⭐ **Open chain shortcut:** $M_{open} = \sum f_i$ (sum of joint freedoms).
- ⭐ **Always include the ground link in $n$** — the #1 KTU mark-loss cause.
- ⭐ **Open chain = large workspace, lower stiffness, multiple IK solutions** (PUMA: 8).
- ⭐ **Closed chain = high stiffness, lower inertia, unique IK inside workspace** (Stewart, Delta).
- ⭐ **Hybrid manipulators** (SCARA arm + parallel wrist, Delta with parallelogram legs) must be classified by **primary topology**, with sub-loops noted.
- ⭐ **Classification rule:** $M = 0$ → structure, $M > 0$ → mechanism, $M < 0$ → pre-loaded structure.
- ⭐ **Number of independent loops:** $L = j_1 - n + 1$ (for planar single-DOF mechanisms).
- ⭐ **Singularity condition:** $\det\bigl(\vec{J}(\vec{q})\bigr) = 0$ — the Jacobian loses rank; the manipulator loses 1 or more DOFs instantaneously.
- ⭐ **Stiffness comparison:** Closed chain stiffness $\approx m \cdot k_{strut}$ (parallel); Open chain stiffness $\approx \left(\sum 1/k_i\right)^{-1}$ (series) — closed chain is **always stiffer** for $m \geq 2$.
- ⭐ **Workspace comparison:** For equal link length, the open chain's workspace is **strictly larger** than the closed chain's, but the closed chain's workspace is **more symmetric and predictable**.
- ⭐ **Real-world examples to memorise:**
  - Open chain: PUMA 560, SCARA, KUKA KR series, anthropomorphic arms.
  - Closed chain: Stewart Platform, Delta Robot, Tricept, Hexapod CNC, surgical da Vinci parallel sub-mechanisms.
- ⭐ **Inverse kinematics** of closed chains requires solving **loop-closure distance equations**, often yielding a **quadratic** with at most **2 solutions per loop** — hence **uniqueness** in the workspace.
- ⭐ **Forward kinematics** of open chains is **closed-form and unique** for a given $q$; that of closed chains requires **iterative numerical methods** (Newton–Raphson) or **constraint-based elimination**.
- ⭐ **For examiners:** Always **draw a labelled sketch** for 2–3 extra marks; always **state the formula before substitution**; always **interpret the final DOF number physically**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Robotics (PEMET748)**, the **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mechanical system — typically a rigid body, a linkage, or a robotic manipulator — is defined as the **minimum number of independent real-valued coordinates** (or generalized coordinates) that must be specified in order to completely and uniquely define the configuration (i.e., the instantaneous spatial pose) of the system with respect to a fixed reference frame.

Mathematically, for a rigid body:

- In **three-dimensional (3D) space**, a single free rigid body possesses **6 DOF**: three translational ($T_x, T_y, T_z$) and three rotational ($R_x, R_y, R_z$).
- In a **two-dimensional (planar) space**, a single free rigid body possesses **3 DOF**: two translations ($T_x, T_y$) and one rotation ($R_z$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For a robotic manipulator, the total DOF equals the **number of independently actuated joints** required to place the end-effector at an arbitrary pose within its task workspace. A robot intended for general 3D spatial tasks must have a **minimum of 6 DOF** (3 for position + 3 for orientation).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **hinged door** in your room. The door can swing open and closed, but it cannot move sideways or up/down. So although the door is a rigid body (and *should* have 6 DOF in free space), the hinge **constrains 5 of those DOF**, leaving only **1 DOF** (the angle of swing $\theta$).

Now imagine a **human arm** reaching out to grab a coffee cup:
- **Shoulder joint** (ball-and-socket) — allows 3 independent rotations.
- **Elbow joint** (hinge) — allows 1 rotation.
- **Wrist joint** (complex, 2 axes) — allows 2 rotations.

Summing these, the arm has roughly **3 + 1 + 2 = 6 DOF**, which is exactly the minimum required to place the hand at any arbitrary position **and** orientation in 3D space — that's why we can pour coffee into any cup without spilling!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every joint you add to a robot either **adds a DOF** (gives the robot a new way to move) or **adds a constraint** (removes a DOF). The art of robot design is balancing the two to achieve the desired dexterity.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard metric for a free rigid body in 3D:** **6 DOF** (always, regardless of robot brand).
- **Standard metric for a free rigid body in 2D (plane):** **3 DOF**.
- The **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** is the standard KTU-mandated tool to compute DOF of any planar or spatial mechanism.
- **Actuated DOF** = number of motors/actuators (each actuator typically provides 1 DOF).
- **Passive DOF** = DOF provided by unactuated joints (e.g., caster wheels, compliant grippers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why DOF Matters in Robotics

A robot's **dexterity**, **workspace reachability**, and **task feasibility** are all dictated by its DOF count. KTU's robotics curriculum treats DOF analysis as the **first step** in any manipulator design problem because:

1. **Task Matching:** A pick-and-place task in 2D needs ≥ 3 DOF; a 3D assembly task typically needs ≥ 6 DOF; a redundant task (e.g., avoiding obstacles) may demand 7+ DOF.
2. **Control Complexity:** Each additional DOF requires an additional actuator, sensor, and control loop.
3. **Singularity Avoidance:** More DOF than the task requires gives the controller extra "internal motions" to avoid joint lock-ups.

### 2.2 Joint Types and Their DOF Contributions

Robotic joints are classified by the number of independent relative motions they permit between two connected links.

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF Provided | Common Use in Robots |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (Hinge) | R | **1** | Elbow, shoulder pitch |
| Prismatic (Slider) | P | **1** | Linear gantries, telescoping arms |
| Helical / Screw | H | **1** | Lead screw actuators |
| Cylindrical | C | **2** | (1 rotation + 1 translation) |
| Universal / Hooke | U | **2** | Drive shafts, wrist coupling |
| Spherical / Ball-and-Socket | S | **3** | Human shoulder, hip |
| Planar | E | **3** | Floating platforms |
| Rigid / Weld | W | **0** | Fixed base-link connection |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{free, 3D}} = 6$ | Free rigid body in 3D | Spatial |
| 2 | $\text{DOF}_{\text{free, 2D}} = 3$ | Free rigid body in plane | Planar |
| 3 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Grübler's formula (planar)** | Planar mechanisms |
| 4 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Kutzbach–Grübler (spatial)** | Spatial mechanisms |
| 5 | $F = 6n - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6-i) \, j_i$ | Alternative Kutzbach form | Spatial |
| 6 | $F = 3n - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Equivalent for planar (alt form) | Planar |
| 7 | $n = \text{links}, \; j_i = \text{joints of class } i$ | Variable definitions | Both |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Lockdown (KTU Convention):** In Formulas 3–6, **$n$** is the **total number of links** (including the fixed ground link), and **$j_i$** is the number of joints that provide exactly **$i$** degrees of freedom. The result $M$ is the **mobility** (equivalent to DOF) of the mechanism.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** DOF analysis of suspension systems (typically 5-DOF per wheel) ensures ride comfort and handling.
- **Surgical Robotics:** The da Vinci Surgical System uses **7 DOF** (4 arm + 3 instrument) to provide redundancy for tremor filtering.
- **Industrial Manipulators:** A standard 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., FANUC M-2000) has **6 DOF**, matching the 6 DOF of a free rigid body for full spatial dexterity.
- **Mobile Robots:** A car-like mobile robot has only **2 DOF** (steering + driving) plus 1 passive DOF for non-holonomic constraint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a Free Rigid Body in 3D Space

A rigid body in 3D space is fully described by the position of its centroid and its orientation in space.

**Step 1 — Position of the centroid:** A point in 3D requires 3 independent Cartesian coordinates:
$$x, \quad y, \quad z \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 \text{ translational coordinates.}$$

**Step 2 — Orientation of the body:** Orientation can be expressed using, e.g., roll–pitch–yaw Euler angles:
$$\phi \; (\text{roll}), \quad \theta \; (\text{pitch}), \quad \psi \; (\text{yaw}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 \text{ rotational coordinates.}$$

**Step 3 — Total count:** The body has no internal flexibility (it is *rigid*), so the total number of independent coordinates is:
$$\text{DOF}_{\text{3D}} = 3 \; (\text{translations}) + 3 \; (\text{rotations}) = 6.$$

**Step 4 — Planar reduction:** Restricting motion to the $xy$-plane:
- Translations: $x, y$ → 2
- Rotations: $\theta$ (about $z$-axis) → 1
- Total: $3$ DOF.

### 3.2 Derivation: Grübler's Formula (Planar Case)

**Step 1 — Count unconstrained bodies:** A free planar rigid body has 3 DOF. If we have $n$ links, the total unconstrained DOF is:
$$N_{\text{free}} = 3n.$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the ground:** One link (the ground/frame) is fixed, so it does not contribute to mobility. Subtract 3:
$$N_{\text{constrained}} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1).$$

**Step 3 — Subtract constraints imposed by joints:** Each joint of class $i$ removes $(3 - i)$ DOF (since it permits only $i$ of the 3 possible planar motions). For a 1-DOF joint (e.g., revolute, prismatic), constraints = $3 - 1 = 2$. For a 2-DOF joint (e.g., planar cam), constraints = $3 - 2 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Final mobility:**
$$M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - 1 \cdot j_2.$$

Renaming the 2-DOF joint term:
$$\boxed{M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2.}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Kutzbach–Grübler Formula (Spatial Case)

**Step 1 — Free bodies in 3D:** $n$ free rigid bodies give $6n$ DOF.

**Step 2 — Ground constraint:** Ground is fixed, so subtract 6: $6n - 6 = 6(n-1)$.

**Step 3 — Joint constraints:** A joint of class $i$ provides $i$ DOF, so it removes $(6 - i)$ DOF. Therefore:
$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5} (6-i) \, j_i.$$

**Step 4 — Expand the summation:**
$$M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5.$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 1: Four-Bar Planar Linkage

**Problem:** Find the mobility of a planar four-bar linkage (4 links, 4 revolute joints).

**Given:** $n = 4$ links (3 moving + 1 ground), $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute, each 1-DOF), $j_2 = 0$.

**Solution using Grübler's formula:**
$$M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2.$$

Substituting:
$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0.$$

Compute term by term:
$$M = 3(3) - 8 - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1.$$

**Result:** The four-bar linkage has **1 DOF** (you can drive it with one input crank). ✔ Verified against the well-known slider-crank and four-bar textbook result.

### 3.5 Worked Example 2: Spatial RR Manipulator (2R Robot Arm)

**Problem:** A robot arm consists of a fixed base, link 1 (revolute joint to ground), and link 2 (revolute joint to link 1). Find its DOF.

**Given:** $n = 3$ links (base + 2 moving), $j_1 = 2$ revolute joints, $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Solution using Kutzbach–Grübler formula:**
$$M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5.$$

Substituting:
$$M = 6(3 - 1) - 5(2) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0.$$

Compute:
$$M = 6(2) - 10 = 12 - 10 = 2.$$

**Result:** The 2R planar-like spatial arm has **2 DOF** ✔ — physically correct, since it can sweep a 2D curve in space (both joints rotate in parallel planes).

### 3.6 Worked Example 3: Standard 6R Industrial Robot

**Problem:** A standard articulated robot has 6 revolute joints connecting 7 links (including ground). Find its DOF.

**Given:** $n = 7$, $j_1 = 6$, all other $j_i = 0$.

**Solution:**
$$M = 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) = 6(6) - 30 = 36 - 30 = 6.$$

**Result:** **6 DOF** ✔ — matches the universal minimum for full 3D spatial dexterity.

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix (Quick Lookup)

| Mechanism | $n$ | Joints | Formula Used | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4-bar linkage | 4 | 4R | Planar Grübler | **1** |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 3R + 1P | Planar Grübler | **1** |
| Five-bar linkage | 5 | 5R | Planar Grübler | **4** |
| 2R spatial arm | 3 | 2R | Spatial K-G | **2** |
| 3R spatial arm | 4 | 3R | Spatial K-G | **3** |
| 6R industrial robot | 7 | 6R | Spatial K-G | **6** |
| SCARA robot (RRP) | 4 | 2R + 1P | Planar Grübler | **3** |

> [!NOTE]
> **SCARA (Selective Compliance Articulated Robot Arm)** is a planar-like 4-DOF robot whose gross positioning is 3 DOF (R-R-P) plus a 1-DOF wrist rotation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Joint DOF Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic Joint] --> B{Number of DOF?}
    B -->|0| W[Rigid / Weld]
    B -->|1| C[Single DOF Joints]
    B -->|2| D[Two DOF Joints]
    B -->|3| E[Three DOF Joints]
    C --> R[Revolute R]
    C --> P[Prismatic P]
    C --> H[Helical H]
    D --> U[Universal U]
    D --> Cy[Cylindrical C]
    E --> S[Spherical S]
    E --> Pl[Planar E]
```

### 4.2 DOF Decomposition of a Rigid Body in 3D

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RB[Free Rigid Body in 3D] --> T[Translations]
    RB --> Ro[Rotations]
    T --> TX[TX along X]
    T --> TY[TY along Y]
    T --> TZ[TZ along Z]
    Ro --> RX[Roll about X]
    Ro --> RY[Pitch about Y]
    Ro --> RZ[Yaw about Z]
    TX --> SUM[Total DOF equals 6]
    TY --> SUM
    TZ --> SUM
    RX --> SUM
    RY --> SUM
    RZ --> SUM
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 6-DOF Industrial Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base[Base Link Ground] -->|J1 Revolute| L1[Link 1 Shoulder]
    L1 -->|J2 Revolute| L2[Link 2 Upper Arm]
    L2 -->|J3 Revolute| L3[Link 3 Elbow]
    L3 -->|J4 Revolute| L4[Link 4 Forearm]
    L4 -->|J5 Revolute| L5[Link 5 Wrist]
    L5 -->|J6 Revolute| EE[End Effector Tool]
    EE --> Task[Arbitrary 3D Pose Position and Orientation]
    CTRL[Controller] -.->|Actuates| J1
    CTRL -.->|Actuates| J2
    CTRL -.->|Actuates| J3
    CTRL -.->|Actuates| J4
    CTRL -.->|Actuates| J5
    CTRL -.->|Actuates| J6
```

### 4.4 DOF Counting Workflow (Engineering Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Mechanism] --> Q1{Motion confined to plane?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Planar[Use Planar Grubler Formula]
    Q1 -->|No| Spatial[Use Spatial Kutzbach Grubler Formula]
    Planar --> P1[Count links n]
    P1 --> P2[Count 1DOF joints j1]
    P2 --> P3[Count 2DOF joints j2]
    P3 --> POut[M equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    Spatial --> S1[Count links n]
    S1 --> S2[Tabulate joints by class ji]
    S2 --> SOut[M equals 6 of n minus 1 minus sum of 6 minus i times ji]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Degree of Freedom" of a robot manipulator. State the DOF of a free rigid body in 3D space.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator is the **number of independent movements** (translations and/or rotations) that the manipulator can perform, or equivalently, the **number of independent coordinates** required to specify the configuration of all its links relative to a fixed base frame.

**Rigid Body DOF (1 Mark):**
A single free rigid body in 3-dimensional space possesses **6 DOF**, namely:
- 3 translations along the $x, y, z$ axes.
- 3 rotations about the $x, y, z$ axes (roll, pitch, yaw).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List any four types of robotic joints and state the number of DOF contributed by each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Joint Type | DOF Contributed |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Revolute (R) — Hinge | 1 |
| 2 | Prismatic (P) — Slider | 1 |
| 3 | Spherical (S) — Ball-and-socket | 3 |
| 4 | Universal (U) — Hooke | 2 |

(Any 4 with correct DOF awarded full 3 marks.)

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** State and derive the **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** for the mobility of a spatial mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Kutzbach–Grübler criterion, calculate the **degrees of freedom** of a **3R spatial manipulator** having 4 links (including the ground) and 3 revolute joints. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A (a): [7 Marks]**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
The Kutzbach–Grübler criterion gives the mobility $M$ (DOF) of a spatial mechanism as:
$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6-i)\,j_i = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 - 3 j_3 - 2 j_4 - j_5,$$
where $n$ = number of links and $j_i$ = number of joints of class $i$ (joints with $i$ DOF).

**Derivation (6 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Each unconstrained rigid body in 3D has **6 DOF**, so $n$ unconstrained links have $6n$ DOF.

**Step 2:** The **ground link** is fixed and has no mobility. Subtracting its 6 DOF yields:
$$6n - 6 = 6(n-1).$$

**Step 3:** A joint of class $i$ permits $i$ independent relative motions and hence imposes $(6-i)$ constraints on the connected bodies. For $j_i$ such joints, the total constraints removed are:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{5}(6-i)\,j_i.$$

**Step 4:** Subtracting the constraints from the residual mobility:
$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6-i)\,j_i.$$

**Step 5:** Expanding:
$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5.$$

---

**Solution to Q.A (b): [7 Marks]**

**Given:** 3R spatial manipulator → $n = 4$ links (base + 3 moving), $j_1 = 3$ (three revolute joints, each 1-DOF), $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

**Apply the Kutzbach–Grübler formula:**
$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5.$$

**Substitute values:**
$$M = 6(4 - 1) - 5(3) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0.$$

**Simplify:**
$$M = 6 \times 3 - 15 = 18 - 15 = 3.$$

**Result:** The 3R spatial manipulator has **3 DOF**. ✔

**Physical Verification (1 Mark):** Three independently actuated revolute joints can orient the end-effector at any point on a 3D spatial surface, which requires exactly 3 independent coordinates — confirming the analytical result.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** What is the **Grübler's formula** for a planar mechanism? Define all the variables used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planar **slider-crank mechanism** consists of 4 links and 4 joints: 3 revolute joints and 1 prismatic joint. Calculate its degrees of freedom using Grübler's formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B (a): [7 Marks]**

**Formula (3 Marks):**
The Grübler formula for the mobility $M$ of a planar mechanism is:
$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2,$$
where:
- $M$ = mobility (degrees of freedom) of the mechanism.
- $n$ = total number of links, **including the fixed ground link**.
- $j_1$ = number of lower-pair joints (1-DOF joints), e.g., revolute and prismatic.
- $j_2$ = number of higher-pair joints (2-DOF joints), e.g., cam-follower in pure rolling.

**Explanation of the terms (4 Marks):**
- The factor **3** comes from the 3 DOF of a free planar rigid body (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- Subtracting 1 link accounts for the **fixed ground**.
- Each 1-DOF joint removes **2 DOF** (since a free body has 3 motions, but a 1-DOF joint allows only 1, so it constrains 2).
- Each 2-DOF joint removes **1 DOF** (allows 2 of 3 motions).
- The result $M$ is the **net mobility** — the number of independent inputs the mechanism requires to have a deterministic motion.

---

**Solution to Q.B (b): [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Number of links: $n = 4$ (crank, connecting rod, slider, ground).
- Revolute joints: 3 → all are 1-DOF, so contribute to $j_1$.
- Prismatic joint: 1 → also 1-DOF, contributes to $j_1$.
- Total: $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$.

**Apply Grübler's formula:**
$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2.$$

**Substitute:**
$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(4) - 0.$$

**Simplify:**
$$M = 3 \times 3 - 8 = 9 - 8 = 1.$$

**Result:** The slider-crank mechanism has **1 DOF**. ✔

**Physical Verification (1 Mark):** A single rotation of the crank produces a deterministic, fully-defined motion of the connecting rod and slider — consistent with 1 DOF.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in DOF problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link:** Many students count only the moving links in $n$. **Always include the fixed ground link** in your link count.
> 2. **Mixing planar and spatial formulas:** If the problem says *planar* (or shows all motion in one plane), use $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. If 3D, use $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$. Using the wrong one is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Mis-classifying joints:** A **revolute** joint is 1-DOF; a **universal** joint is 2-DOF; a **ball-and-socket** is 3-DOF. Mis-counting is a frequent error.
> 4. **Missing units/dimensions in final answer:** Always state the **numerical value** AND say "DOF" (no units, but state it explicitly).
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps in Part B sub-parts (a):** Examiners allocate marks for **each derivation step**. Writing only the final formula will yield partial (≤ 2/7) credit.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **DOF = independent coordinates** to fully specify a mechanism's configuration.
- ✅ A free rigid body in **3D = 6 DOF**; in **2D (plane) = 3 DOF**.
- ✅ **Grübler's formula (planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ✅ **Kutzbach–Grübler (spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ Always include the **ground link** in $n$.
- ✅ **Joint DOF quick-recall:** R = 1, P = 1, H = 1, C = 2, U = 2, S = 3, E = 3, W = 0.
- ✅ A general-purpose 3D task robot needs **≥ 6 DOF**; > 6 DOF implies **kinematic redundancy**.
- ✅ **Worked benchmarks to memorize:** 4-bar = 1 DOF, slider-crank = 1 DOF, 6R industrial robot = 6 DOF, SCARA gross motion = 3 DOF.
- ✅ **Trap formulas to avoid:** Never write Grübler's formula with a leading factor of 6 for a planar problem — that's the most common KTU error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Configurations - PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Configurations - PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Robot Configurations — PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Robot Configuration?

In robotics, the **configuration** of a manipulator is defined by the **ordered sequence of joint types** that connect the **base** (mounting plate) to the **end-effector** (gripper/tool). Each joint contributes either a **translational (linear)** or **rotational (angular)** degree of freedom, and the combination of these joints completely defines the **kinematic structure**, the **shape of the workspace**, and the **class of tasks** the robot can perform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET748, Module 1):**
> *"Robot anatomy and working volume – Robot configurations: PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR and their work envelopes. Classification based on coordinate system."*

KTU follows the **two-letter pair notation system** for any general configuration, where:

- **P** → **Prismatic joint** (also called *Linear* or *Sliding* joint). Motion is pure **translation** along a single axis. The joint variable is a **linear displacement** $d$ measured in **metres (m)** or **millimetres (mm)**.
- **R** → **Revolute joint** (also called *Rotary* or *Hinge* joint). Motion is pure **rotation** about a single axis. The joint variable is an **angular displacement** $\theta$ measured in **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the configuration string:**
> The letters are written from **base → wrist**. So, "RPP" means the **first joint (waist) is Revolute**, and the next **two joints (shoulder & elbow) are Prismatic**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine you are reaching for a coffee mug on a high shelf:

| Robot Joint | Human Counterpart | Motion Type |
|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Joint 1 (Base rotation) | Shoulder swivel (turning your torso left/right) | **Revolute (R)** |
| Joint 2 (Shoulder lift) | Raising your upper arm up/down | **Revolute (R)** |
| Joint 3 (Elbow bend) | Folding/unfolding your forearm | **Revolute (R)** |

Your **3R (RRR)** arm can sweep through a huge region of space. Now imagine a **forklift**: the cab rotates (R), the mast goes up/down (P), and the forks slide in/out (P) — that is a classic **RPP** configuration! The joint sequence directly controls the **shape of the reachable region** (the **work envelope**).

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Joint Types

**Prismatic Joint (P) — Linear Slider**
- A prismatic joint permits **pure linear translation** along a single axis.
- Kinematic variable: $d$ (linear displacement).
- Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters for a pure P-joint: $\theta$ = constant, $d$ = variable, $r$ = constant, $\alpha$ = constant (typically 0).
- The relative transformation is: a pure translation vector.
- **Analogy:** A drawer sliding in/out of a cabinet.

**Revolute Joint (R) — Rotary Hinge**
- A revolute joint permits **pure rotation** about a single fixed axis.
- Kinematic variable: $\theta$ (angular displacement).
- DH parameters: $\theta$ = variable, $d$ = constant, $r$ = constant, $\alpha$ = constant (typically 0).
- The relative transformation involves a rotation matrix about the joint axis.
- **Analogy:** A door swinging open/closed on its hinge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Robots by Configuration

The four configurations required by KTU Module 1 (PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR) are listed below in order of **increasing kinematic complexity** and **workspace curvature**. Each is tied to a **standard coordinate-system geometry** which gives the robot its common name.

| Configuration | Common Name | Coordinate Geometry | DOF | Workspace Shape |
|---------------|-------------|---------------------|-----|-----------------|
| **PPP** | Cartesian / Gantry Robot | Cartesian $(x, y, z)$ | 3 | Rectangular box (cuboid) |
| **RPP** | Cylindrical Robot | Cylindrical $(r, \phi, z)$ | 3 | Hollow cylinder |
| **RRP** | Spherical / Polar Robot | Spherical $(\theta, \phi, r)$ | 3 | Spherical sector |
| **RRR** | Articulated / Anthropomorphic | Articulated (joint coords) | 3 | Spherical shell / torus-like |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOF Rule:** A **3-DOF** planar positioning robot is obtained by cascading **3 independent joints**. Adding a **4th joint** (e.g., a wrist roll) provides orientation. The configurations PPP/RPP/RRP/RRR here all address **3-DOF positioning arms**; the **wrist (orientation)** is added separately in a later module.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Robot Configurations

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The joint ranges and link lengths are *not* fixed KTU constants. They depend on the **manufacturer's mechanical design**. The equations below are the *general* kinematic relations used to compute the work envelope. KTU expects you to write them in terms of the **general joint variables** with their **travel limits** explicitly stated.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Number of independent DOF | $\text{DOF} = (\text{number of joints})$ for serial chain | dimensionless |
| 2 | PPP — forward kinematics | $x = d_1,\;\; y = d_2,\;\; z = d_3$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | PPP — work envelope (box) | $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$ | $m^3$ |
| 4 | RPP — forward kinematics | $r = d_2,\;\; \phi = \theta_1,\;\; z = d_3$ | m, rad, m |
| 5 | RPP — work envelope (cylinder) | $V = \pi (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2) \, z_{\max}$ | $m^3$ |
| 6 | RRP — forward kinematics | $r = d_3,\;\; \phi = \theta_2,\;\; \alpha = \theta_1$ | m, rad, rad |
| 7 | RRP — work envelope (sector) | $V = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi r_{\max}^3 (1 - \cos\alpha_{\max})$ | $m^3$ |
| 8 | RRR — link lengths | $l_1, l_2, l_3$ (arm lengths) | metres (m) |
| 9 | RRR — radial reach | $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ | metres (m) |
| 10 | RRR — total reach | $r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2 + l_3$ | metres (m) |
| 11 | RRR — minimum reach | $r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - (l_2 + l_3) \vert$ (use `\vert` in tables) | metres (m) |
| 12 | Joint limit (P) | $0 \le d_i \le d_{i,\max}$ | metres (m) |
| 13 | Joint limit (R) | $\theta_{i,\min} \le \theta_i \le \theta_{i,\max}$ | radians (rad) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical bar** `\|` in markdown tables — otherwise the table syntax breaks. KTU papers are typed in LaTeX, but the same rule applies when students write their answer sheets.

## 2.3 Detailed Configuration Analysis

### 2.3.1 PPP — Cartesian (Gantry) Robot

**Structure (base → end-effector):**
1. **Joint 1:** Prismatic — slides along the **X-axis**.
2. **Joint 2:** Prismatic — slides along the **Y-axis**.
3. **Joint 3:** Prismatic — slides along the **Z-axis**.

**Why this design exists:** Three orthogonal linear axes make the **forward kinematics trivial** — the joint displacements *are* the Cartesian coordinates. There are **no trigonometric functions** in the position equations, which makes PPP ideal for **high-precision pick-and-place**.

**Forward Kinematics:**

$$x = d_1$$

$$y = d_2$$

$$z = d_3$$

**Work Envelope:** A **rectangular parallelepiped (box)** of dimensions $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$, where each $L_i$ is the travel range of the corresponding prismatic joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Gantry variant:** When the structure is mounted on an **overhead gantry frame** (linear rails on the ceiling), it is called a *Gantry robot*. These are common in **CNC machining, palletising, and large-panel handling** in the aerospace and woodworking industries.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Machine loading/unloading in CNC cells.
- PCB assembly and inspection.
- Palletising boxes on conveyors.
- **Strongest suit:** Highest positional accuracy and repeatability of all 4 configurations (no serial bending moments on the links).

**Limitations:**
- Very large **footprint** (workspace volume = envelope volume).
- Cannot reach **around obstacles** — the axes are mutually perpendicular and rigid.
- Mechanical rigidity requires heavy structural members.

### 2.3.2 RPP — Cylindrical Robot

**Structure (base → end-effector):**
1. **Joint 1:** Revolute — rotates the entire arm about a **vertical axis** (waist rotation, angle $\theta_1$).
2. **Joint 2:** Prismatic — slides the arm **radially** in/out (radial extension, $d_2$).
3. **Joint 3:** Prismatic — slides the end-effector **vertically** up/down (height, $d_3$).

**Forward Kinematics (Cylindrical Coordinates):**

$$r = d_2, \quad \phi = \theta_1, \quad z = d_3$$

**Conversion to Cartesian:**

$$x = r \cos\phi = d_2 \cos\theta_1$$

$$y = r \sin\phi = d_2 \sin\theta_1$$

$$z = d_3$$

**Work Envelope:** A **hollow cylinder** (or "thick-walled tube") of:
- inner radius $r_{\min}$,
- outer radius $r_{\max}$,
- height $z_{\max}$.

$$V_{\text{cyl}} = \pi (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2) \, z_{\max}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "cylindrical"?** The position of the end-effector is most naturally described using the **cylindrical coordinate system** $(r, \phi, z)$ — hence the name.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Classic **Unimate** and **FANUC** cylindrical-arm robots in the 1970s–80s.
- Machine tending (lathe loading), die casting, foundry work.
- **Strongest suit:** Compact floor footprint; good vertical reach.

### 2.3.3 RRP — Spherical (Polar) Robot

**Structure (base → end-effector):**
1. **Joint 1:** Revolute — rotates the arm about a **vertical axis** (waist, $\theta_1$).
2. **Joint 2:** Revolute — pitches the arm **up/down** from horizontal (shoulder elevation, $\theta_2$).
3. **Joint 3:** Prismatic — slides the end-effector **radially** along the arm (telescope, $d_3$).

**Forward Kinematics (Spherical Coordinates):**

$$r = d_3, \quad \phi = \theta_1, \quad \alpha = \theta_2$$

**Conversion to Cartesian:**

$$x = r \cos\alpha \cos\phi$$

$$y = r \cos\alpha \sin\phi$$

$$z = r \sin\alpha$$

**Work Envelope:** A **spherical sector** (portion of a thick spherical shell) bounded by:
- $0 \le r \le r_{\max}$,
- $0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$,
- $-\alpha_{\max} \le \alpha \le +\alpha_{\max}$.

$$V_{\text{sph}} = \frac{2}{3} \pi r_{\max}^3 (1 - \cos\alpha_{\max})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical note (frequently asked in KTU viva):** The **Unimate** (1961) — the first industrial robot — was a **polar (RRP)** configuration. KTU examiners love this fact.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Early robotic welding, forging, paint spraying.
- NASA remote manipulators (space-rated polar arms).
- **Strongest suit:** Large spherical workspace relative to floor footprint.

### 2.3.4 RRR — Articulated (Anthropomorphic) Robot

**Structure (base → end-effector):**
1. **Joint 1:** Revolute — waist rotation about a **vertical axis** ($\theta_1$).
2. **Joint 2:** Revolute — shoulder pitch about a **horizontal axis** ($\theta_2$).
3. **Joint 3:** Revolute — elbow pitch about a **parallel horizontal axis** ($\theta_3$).

**Forward Kinematics (DH Convention):**

Using Denavit–Hartenberg parameters with link lengths $l_1$ (shoulder offset from waist), $l_2$ (upper arm), $l_3$ (forearm):

$$x = (l_2 \cos\theta_2 + l_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \cos\theta_1$$

$$y = (l_2 \cos\theta_2 + l_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)) \sin\theta_1$$

$$z = l_1 + l_2 \sin\theta_2 + l_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$

**Work Envelope:** A **partial spherical shell** with:
- Outer reach (fully extended): $r_{\max} = l_1 + l_2 + l_3$.
- Inner reach (fully folded): $r_{\min} = \vert l_1 - (l_2 + l_3) \vert$ (written as `\vert l_1 - (l_2 + l_3) \vert` to avoid markdown conflicts).
- Vertical range determined by shoulder pitch limits.

The envelope is a **thick spherical shell** (with a hollow region near the base).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "anthropomorphic"?** The three revolute joints mimic the **waist–shoulder–elbow** anatomy of a human arm. Hence the alternate name: *human-arm robot*. Examples: **ABB IRB, KUKA KR, FANUC ArcMate, Yaskawa Motoman**.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Arc welding, assembly, painting, material handling.
- **Dominant configuration in modern industry** (>70% of all industrial robots shipped).
- **Strongest suit:** Maximum dexterity; can reach into cavities, around obstacles, and orient tools freely (with a 3-axis wrist).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

| Configuration | Industry Use-Case | Why Preferred |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **PPP** | CNC loading, PCB pick-and-place, large-panel handling | Highest accuracy; orthogonal axes; overhead gantry |
| **RPP** | Die casting, machine tending, injection moulding | Compact footprint; good vertical reach |
| **RRP** | Forging, early welding, space manipulators | Spherical workspace suits high-temperature cells |
| **RRR** | Automotive welding, painting, assembly, palletising | Maximum dexterity; obstacle avoidance; human-like motion |

## 2.5 Comparison Snapshot (Last-Mile Revision)

| Property | PPP | RPP | RRP | RRR |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Coordinate System | Cartesian $(x,y,z)$ | Cylindrical $(r,\phi,z)$ | Spherical $(r,\phi,\alpha)$ | Joint $(\theta_1,\theta_2,\theta_3)$ |
| Workspace | Box | Hollow cylinder | Spherical sector | Spherical shell |
| Mechanical Complexity | Low | Medium | High | Highest |
| Accuracy | Highest | High | Medium | Lower (servo errors add up) |
| Dexterity | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Cost | Medium (linear guides) | Low | Medium | Highest |
| Common Manufacturer | Aerotech, Parker | Unimate, older Cincinnati Milacron | Unimate, NASA RMS | ABB, KUKA, FANUC, Yaskawa |
