L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ ✅ **This matches the result from Method 1** — confirming both methods are equivalent. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Joint Types vs. Jacobian Columns | Joint Type | Linear Velocity Component ($J_{vi}$) | Angular Velocity Component ($J_{\omega i}$) | |------------|--------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------| | **Revolute** | $z_{i-1} \times (p_{ee} - p_{i-1})$ | $z_{i-1}$ | | **Prismatic** | $z_{i-1}$ | $\mathbf{0}$ (zero vector) | --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Inverse Velocity **Given:** A 2R planar arm with $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m, current joint angles $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$. **Desired end-effector velocity:** $\dot{X} = [0.5,\, 0.5]^T$ m/s. **Step 1 — Compute Jacobian at given config:** $s_1 = \sin 30° = 0.5$, $c_1 = \cos 30° = 0.866$ $s_{12} = \sin 75° = 0.966$, $c_{12} = \cos 75° = 0.259$ $$ J = \begin{bmatrix} -(1)(0.5) - (1)(0.966) & -(1)(0.966) \\ (1)(0.866) + (1)(0.259) & (1)(0.259) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & -0.966 \\ 1.125 & 0.259 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 — Compute det(J):** $$ \det(J) = (-1.466)(0.259) - (-0.966)(1.125) = -0.380 + 1.087 = 0.707 \neq 0 $$ ✅ Non-singular — inverse exists. **Step 3 — Compute Inverse Jacobian:** $$ J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.707}\begin{bmatrix} 0.259 & 0.966 \\ -1.125 & -1.466 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.366 & 1.366 \\ -1.591 & -2.073 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Joint Velocities:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta_1} \\ \dot{\theta_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.366 & 1.366 \\ -1.591 & -2.073 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 \\ -1.832 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s} $$ **Result:** Joint 1 must rotate at **0.866 rad/s** and Joint 2 at **−1.832 rad/s** to achieve the desired EE velocity. --- ## 3.5 Singularity Derivation (2R Planar) The manipulator is singular when det(J) = 0: $$ \det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 0° \text{ or } 180° $$ **Physical Meaning:** When $\theta_2 = 0$, the arm is **fully stretched out** (or folded back). At this configuration, the end-effector cannot move in the radial direction — it has only **tangential motion**, losing 1 DOF. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Jacobian Velocity Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Joint Velocity Vector q-dot
n x 1"] -->|"J(q) Mapping"| B["Jacobian Matrix
6 x n Function of q"] B --> C["End Effector Velocity V
6 x 1 vector"] C --> D["Linear Velocity v
3 x 1"] C --> E["Angular Velocity omega
3 x 1"] D --> F["Resolved Rate Controller
Industrial Robot"] E --> F F --> G["Joint Torque Command
tau = J transpose F"] G --> H["Actuator Motors"] H -.Feedback.-> A ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Deriving and Applying the Jacobian ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Assign DH Parameters
Identify Joint Types R or P"] --> S2["Step 2: Compute Forward Kinematics
Position f of q"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Derive Jacobian
Method A Differentiate
OR Method B Cross Product"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Check Singularity
Compute det J"] S4 -->|"det J non zero"| S5["Step 5: Solve Velocity Kinematics
q dot equals J inverse times V"] S4 -->|"det J equals zero"| S6["Singular Configuration
Loss of DOF
Infinite Joint Velocities Required"] S5 --> S7["Step 6: Send Commands to Actuators
Real time Control Loop"] S7 -. Feedback .-> S2 ``` ## 4.3 Joint Type Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD J["Joint i"] --> JT{"Type?"} JT -->|"Revolute R"| RJ["J sub i equals
z sub i minus 1 cross p ee minus p sub i minus 1
semi column
z sub i minus 1"] JT -->|"Prismatic P"| PJ["J sub i equals
z sub i minus 1
semi column
zero vector"] RJ --> ASS["Assemble into 6 x n matrix J"] PJ --> ASS ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: Joint Space ↔ Task Space ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph JS["Joint Space"] Q1["q1"] Q2["q2"] Q3["q dot n"] end subgraph TS["Task Space Cartesian"] V1["v linear"] V2["omega angular"] end JS -- "J q" --> TS TS -- "J inverse V" --> JS JS -. "Null Space
Redundant DOFs" .-> JS ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - Each block represents a **functional transformation**, not a physical component. > - The feedback loop represents **real-time closed-loop control** used in industrial robots. > - The null-space loop applies only to redundant manipulators (n > 6). # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the Jacobian matrix of a robotic manipulator. Mention its dimensions for (i) a 6-DOF spatial manipulator, and (ii) a 3-DOF planar manipulator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Jacobian matrix J(q) of a robotic manipulator is the time-derivative of the forward kinematics function and relates the joint-space velocity vector to the end-effector (task-space) velocity vector as **V = J(q) q̇**. - (i) For a 6-DOF spatial manipulator: J is a **6 × 6** square matrix. - (ii) For a 3-DOF planar manipulator: J is a **3 × 3** square matrix. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **What is meant by a singular configuration of a robot manipulator? Write the condition for singularity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A singular configuration is a joint configuration at which the manipulator **loses one or more degrees of freedom** in Cartesian space. Mathematically, it is defined by the condition: $$\det(J(q)) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{rank}(J(q)) < \min(m, n)$$ At a singularity, the manipulator cannot move the end-effector in certain directions, and infinite joint velocities would be required to produce any finite end-effector velocity in those directions. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** (Internal Choice). Each has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. --- ### ✅ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]* #### **Q.A (a) Derive the Jacobian matrix for a 2R planar manipulator using the direct differentiation method.** [7 Marks] **Solution:** **Step 1 — Forward Kinematics Equations:** For a 2R planar arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$: $$ x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) $$ $$ y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) $$ **Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. time:** Differentiating x: $$ \dot{x} = -L_1\sin\theta_1\,\dot{\theta_1} - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,(\dot{\theta_1}+\dot{\theta_2}) $$ Differentiating y: $$ \dot{y} = L_1\cos\theta_1\,\dot{\theta_1} + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,(\dot{\theta_1}+\dot{\theta_2}) $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange by $\dot{\theta_1}$ and $\dot{\theta_2}$:** $$ \dot{x} = \bigl[-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta_1} + \bigl[-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta_2} $$ $$ \dot{y} = \bigl[L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta_1} + \bigl[L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr]\dot{\theta_2} $$ **Step 4 — Express in Matrix Form:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta_1} \\ \dot{\theta_2} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the Jacobian:** $$ J(\theta_1,\theta_2) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} $$ ✅ **Answer:** The Jacobian J is a 2 × 2 matrix for the 2R planar manipulator as derived above. --- #### **Q.A (b) For the same 2R manipulator with $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m, find the joint velocities required to achieve an end-effector velocity of $[0.5,\, 0.5]^T$ m/s when $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$. Also identify the singularity condition.** [7 Marks] **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute trig values:** - $s_1 = \sin 30° = 0.5$, $c_1 = \cos 30° = 0.866$ - $s_{12} = \sin 75° = 0.966$, $c_{12} = \cos 75° = 0.259$ **Step 2 — Substitute into J:** $$ J = \begin{bmatrix} -(1)(0.5) - (1)(0.966) & -(1)(0.966) \\ (1)(0.866) + (1)(0.259) & (1)(0.259) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.466 & -0.966 \\ 1.125 & 0.259 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Compute determinant:** $$ \det(J) = (-1.466)(0.259) - (-0.966)(1.125) = -0.380 + 1.087 = 0.707 $$ Since $\det(J) \neq 0$, the configuration is **non-singular**. Inverse exists. **Step 4 — Compute inverse Jacobian:** $$ J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.707}\begin{bmatrix} 0.259 & 0.966 \\ -1.125 & -1.466 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.366 & 1.366 \\ -1.591 & -2.073 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Compute joint velocities:** $$ \dot{q} = J^{-1} \dot{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.366 & 1.366 \\ -1.591 & -2.073 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} (0.366)(0.5) + (1.366)(0.5) \\ (-1.591)(0.5) + (-2.073)(0.5) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 \\ -1.832 \end{bmatrix} \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 6 — Singularity condition:** Setting $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0$, the manipulator is singular when: $$ \theta_2 = 0° \text{ or } 180° $$ **Physical meaning:** At these configurations the arm is fully stretched or folded back — it loses the ability to move radially. ✅ **Answer:** $\dot{\theta_1} = 0.866$ rad/s, $\dot{\theta_2} = -1.832$ rad/s. Singularity at $\theta_2 = 0°$ or $180°$. --- ### ✅ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]* #### **Q.B (a) Explain the cross-product method of deriving the Jacobian. Show how the column of the Jacobian is obtained for (i) a revolute joint, and (ii) a prismatic joint.** [7 Marks] **Solution:** **Cross-Product Method — Overview:** Instead of differentiating the forward kinematics equations, the Jacobian columns can be obtained directly from the kinematic parameters using screw theory / cross-products of the joint axis vectors and position vectors. **General form of i-th column:** $$ J_i = \begin{bmatrix} J_{vi} \\ J_{\omega i} \end{bmatrix} $$ **(i) For a Revolute Joint i:** The angular velocity contribution is $J_{\omega i} = z_{i-1}$, and the linear velocity is $J_{vi} = z_{i-1} \times (p_{ee} - p_{i-1})$. $$ J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \times (p_{ee} - p_{i-1}) \\ z_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Derivation logic:** A revolute joint rotates about $z_{i-1}$, producing angular velocity $\omega = z_{i-1}\dot{q_i}$. The corresponding linear velocity at the EE is $\omega \times r$, where $r$ is the vector from joint axis to EE. **(ii) For a Prismatic Joint i:** The prismatic joint translates along $z_{i-1}$, contributing only linear velocity: $$ J_i = \begin{bmatrix} z_{i-1} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Derivation logic:** Prismatic joints produce translation, not rotation, hence the angular component is zero. **Final Assembly:** $$ J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} J_1 & J_2 & \cdots & J_n \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times n} $$ ✅ **Answer:** As derived above for both joint types. --- #### **Q.B (b) Explain the relationship between end-effector forces and joint torques using the Jacobian. State the principle used.** [7 Marks] **Solution:** **Principle Used:** The **Principle of Virtual Work** (conservation of energy for infinitesimal displacements). **Statement:** For an ideal manipulator (frictionless, massless), the virtual work done by joint torques equals the virtual work done by the end-effector forces: $$ \delta W_{joint} = \delta W_{task} $$ $$ \tau^T \delta q = F^T \delta X $$ where $\tau$ is the joint torque vector, F is the end-effector force/wrench, $\delta q$ is the virtual joint displacement, and $\delta X$ is the virtual end-effector displacement. **Substituting** $\delta X = J\,\delta q$: $$ \tau^T \delta q = F^T (J\,\delta q) = (J^T F)^T \delta q $$ Equating both sides (since $\delta q$ is arbitrary): $$ \boxed{\tau = J^T(q)\,F} $$ **Physical Interpretation:** - A **force F applied at the end-effector** is mapped into equivalent joint torques via the transpose of the Jacobian. - This is the **dual relationship** of velocity kinematics ($V = J\dot{q}$). - This equation is the foundation of **force control**, **haptic feedback**, and **compliance control** in modern robotics. **Example Application:** In a robotic gripper, the force the gripper applies on an object (F) is converted into motor torques at each joint using $\tau = J^T F$, accounting for the lever-arm geometry captured in J. ✅ **Answer:** The relationship is $\tau = J^T F$, derived from the Principle of Virtual Work. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to include both linear and angular components** in the 6 × n Jacobian for spatial manipulators — full marks require both 3 × 1 halves. > 2. **Sign errors in cross-products** — always use the right-hand rule carefully. > 3. **Confusing J (Jacobian) with J⁻¹ (inverse Jacobian)** — J maps joint→task; J⁻¹ maps task→joint. KTU examiners explicitly check this. > 4. **Not stating det(J) ≠ 0** before inverting J in numerical problems — losing 1–2 marks. > 5. **Using J⁻¹ for redundant manipulators** — must use pseudoinverse $J^+$ for n > 6. > 6. **In the force/torque duality question**, forgetting to mention the **Principle of Virtual Work** as the derivation basis — full marks require naming the principle. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ⚡ Quick-Recall Bullet Points - **Jacobian J(q):** Time-derivative of forward kinematics; **6 × n matrix** for spatial, **3 × n** for planar. - **Forward Velocity Kinematics:** $V = J(q)\dot{q}$ — joint velocity → end-effector velocity. - **Inverse Velocity Kinematics:** $\dot{q} = J^{-1}V$ (only for non-redundant, non-singular). - **Redundant case:** Use pseudoinverse $\dot{q} = J^+ V$. - **Direct differentiation:** $J_{ij} = \partial f_i / \partial q_j$. - **Cross-product method (Revolute):** Column = $[z_{i-1} \times (p_{ee} - p_{i-1}); z_{i-1}]$. - **Cross-product method (Prismatic):** Column = $[z_{i-1}; \mathbf{0}]$. - **Singularity:** $\det(J) = 0$ → loss of DOF, infinite joint velocities. - **2R planar singularity:** $\theta_2 = 0°$ or $180°$ (arm fully extended/folded). - **Static force duality:** $\tau = J^T F$ (derived via Virtual Work). - **Resolved rate motion control** is the practical application of inverse velocity kinematics. - **Manipulability measure:** $\mu = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ — quantifies distance from singularity. ### 🔑 Memory Aid — "JMAPS" | Letter | Meaning | |--------|---------| | **J** | Jacobian maps Joint velocities → EE velocities | | **M** | Matrix dimensions = (task DOF) × (joint DOF) | | **A** | Always check determinant before inversion | | **P** | Pseudoinverse for redundant robots | | **S** | Singularities cause Singularity-problems |

topics.title: Velocity Jacobian and Potential Energy Expression
Euler-Lagrange Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Euler-Lagrange Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler-Lagrange Equation (KTU 2024 Robotics Module 3 Definition)**
>
> The **Euler-Lagrange Equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that yields the extremum (typically the minimum) of a functional. In the context of **robot dynamics**, it provides a systematic, energy-based framework to derive the equations of motion of multi-body mechanical systems — particularly serial-chain robotic manipulators — directly from the system's **kinetic energy (K)** and **potential energy (P)** without explicitly resolving internal constraint forces.

The scalar functional being minimized is the **Action Integral** $\mathcal{S}$, defined as:

$$\mathcal{S} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt$$

where $L = K - P$ is the **Lagrangian** of the system, $q$ represents the **generalized coordinates** (joint angles for revolute joints or joint displacements for prismatic joints), and $\dot{q}$ represents the corresponding generalized velocities.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mountain Skier's Path Analogy**
>
> Imagine a skier standing at the top of a mountain who wants to reach the bottom in the shortest time. Although a straight line down the slope appears short, it may be very steep and dangerous. A slightly curved path — following the **valley contours** — is actually faster and uses less energy.
>
> The Euler-Lagrange equation operates on exactly this principle: it finds the **"natural trajectory"** (the equation of motion) that the system *prefers* — the path along which the **difference between kinetic and potential energy is optimally balanced** over time. For a robotic arm, this means the equation automatically captures gravity, inertia, Coriolis effects, and centrifugal forces simply by reading the energy of each link.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Robotics

The Euler-Lagrange formulation is one of the **two principal methods** used to derive manipulator dynamics (the other being the **Newton-Euler** recursive method). It produces a closed-form, structured **n × n matrix equation** of the form:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q, \dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

This matrix equation is the foundation for **model-based control** strategies such as **Computed Torque Control** and **Adaptive Control**, which are directly tested in KTU university examinations.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Energy-Based?** Unlike Newton's method, which requires free-body diagrams and careful handling of constraint forces at every joint, the Lagrangian method requires only:
> 1. The kinetic energy $K$ of the entire system.
> 2. The potential energy $P$ of the entire system.
> 3. The generalized coordinates $q$ chosen for the system.
>
> The internal joint reaction forces automatically vanish in the derivation — a major advantage for complex, multi-DOF manipulators.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Variational Principle (Hamilton's Principle)

The Euler-Lagrange equation emerges from **Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action**, which states:

> *"Among all possible trajectories connecting the initial configuration $q(t_1)$ to the final configuration $q(t_2)$, the actual path taken by a conservative system is the one that makes the action integral $\mathcal{S}$ stationary (i.e., $\delta \mathcal{S} = 0$)."*

Mathematically:

$$\delta \mathcal{S} = \delta \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt = 0$$

## 2.2 The Euler-Lagrange Equation — General Form

For a system described by a single generalized coordinate $q(t)$, the scalar Euler-Lagrange equation is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = Q_{nc}$$

where $Q_{nc}$ represents any **non-conservative generalized forces** (such as joint motor torques). For robotic manipulators where motor torques are the only non-conservative inputs:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \tau_i$$

### 2.2.1 Extension to Multi-DOF Systems

For an **n-DOF manipulator** with generalized coordinate vector $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$, we obtain a **vector Euler-Lagrange equation**:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \tau$$

where each component $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ satisfies:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Derivation Logic

The Euler-Lagrange method follows a structured 6-step pipeline for any robotic manipulator:

1. **Identify Generalized Coordinates** — Choose $n$ independent coordinates $q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n$ (typically joint angles $\theta_i$ for revolute joints).
2. **Compute Kinetic Energy** — Sum the kinetic energies of all links: $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2} \omega_i^T I_i \omega_i$.
3. **Compute Potential Energy** — Sum gravitational potential energies: $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g h_{c_i}$.
4. **Form the Lagrangian** — Compute $L = K - P$.
5. **Apply the Euler-Lagrange Operator** — For each $i$, compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$.
6. **Equate to Generalized Forces** — Set the result equal to the applied torque $\tau_i$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU University Exams.**

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Standard Form / Equation** | **Units (SI)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Lagrangian | $L = K - P$ | Joules (J) |
| $\mathcal{S}$ | Action integral | $\mathcal{S} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L \, dt$ | J·s |
| $q_i$ | Generalized coordinate | Joint angle $\theta_i$ or displacement $d_i$ | rad or m |
| $\dot{q}_i$ | Generalized velocity | Time derivative of $q_i$ | rad/s or m/s |
| $\tau_i$ | Generalized force/torque | Actuator torque at joint $i$ | N·m or N |
| $K_i$ | KE of link $i$ | $\frac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_i \omega_i^2$ | J |
| $P_i$ | PE of link $i$ | $m_i g h_{c_i}$ | J |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ | Generalized momentum conjugate | — | kg·m²/s |
| $M(q)$ | Mass/Inertia matrix | $n \times n$ symmetric, positive definite | kg·m² |
| $C(q,\dot{q})$ | Coriolis/centrifugal matrix | $n \times n$ matrix | kg·m²/s |
| $G(q)$ | Gravity vector | $n \times 1$ vector | N·m |
| **Full Dynamic Model** | **Closed-form dynamics** | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Euler-Lagrange model is the **backbone** of advanced robotic control:

- **Computed Torque Control (CTC)**: Computes $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ — requires full dynamic model.
- **Adaptive Control**: Updates $M$, $C$, $G$ estimates online when payload changes.
- **Trajectory Planning**: Verifies that planned joint trajectories obey physical laws.
- **Space Robotics**: Used by NASA/JAXA for free-floating manipulator arms (satellite servicing).
- **Humanoid Robots**: ASIMO, Atlas, and Optimus use Lagrangian dynamics for whole-body motion planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of $M(q)$:** The mass matrix $M(q)$ is always **symmetric** and **positive definite** for any valid manipulator configuration. This property is exploited in Lyapunov-based stability proofs frequently tested in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Euler-Lagrange Equation (Single DOF)

**Step 1 — Start with the Action Integral**

$$\mathcal{S} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Consider a Varied Path**

Let the actual trajectory be $q(t)$. A neighboring varied path is $\tilde{q}(t) = q(t) + \epsilon \eta(t)$, where $\epsilon$ is a small scalar parameter and $\eta(t)$ is a smooth function that vanishes at the endpoints ($\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$).

**Step 3 — Impose Stationarity**

Setting $\delta \mathcal{S} = 0$:

$$\delta \mathcal{S} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} \delta q + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \delta \dot{q} \right] dt = 0$$

Since $\delta q = \epsilon \eta$ and $\delta \dot{q} = \epsilon \dot{\eta}$:

$$\delta \mathcal{S} = \epsilon \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} \eta + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \right] dt = 0$$

**Step 4 — Integration by Parts on the Second Term**

Apply integration by parts to $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \, dt$:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \, dt = \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \eta \right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \eta \, dt$$

Because $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \, dt = - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \eta \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Combine and Apply the Fundamental Lemma**

Substituting back:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \right] \eta(t) \, dt = 0$$

Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary (by the **Fundamental Lemma of the Calculus of Variations**), the bracketed term must vanish identically:

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = 0}$$

For non-conservative forces $Q$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = Q$$

This is the **Euler-Lagrange Equation** in its canonical form.

## 3.2 Worked Example — 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU Favourite):** Consider a 2-link planar manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$, link lengths $l_1, l_2$, joint angles $q_1, q_2$ (measured from horizontal), and point masses at the end of each link. Gravity $g$ acts downward. Derive the equations of motion using Euler-Lagrange formulation.

### Step 1 — Position of Each Mass

Position of mass 1 (end of link 1):

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos q_1, \quad y_1 = l_1 \sin q_1$$

Position of mass 2 (end of link 2):

$$x_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

### Step 2 — Velocities

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{y}_1 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

### Step 3 — Kinetic Energy

Squared speed of mass 1:

$$v_1^2 = \dot{x}_1^2 + \dot{y}_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

Squared speed of mass 2:

$$v_2^2 = (l_1 \dot{q}_1)^2 + (l_2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2))^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

Total kinetic energy:

$$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[ l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2 \right]$$

### Step 4 — Potential Energy

Taking the reference at $y = 0$:

$$P_1 = m_1 g l_1 \sin q_1$$

$$P_2 = m_2 g \left[ l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \right]$$

Total:

$$P = (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \sin q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

### Step 5 — Lagrangian

$$L = K - P$$

### Step 6 — Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation for $q_1$

First, compute partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1} = -(m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 - m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

For the conjugate momentum term, compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}$ by inspection of $K$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \cos q_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2 \cos q_2$$

Simplify:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 \dot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \cos q_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2 \cos q_2$$

Take total time derivative (using $\frac{d}{dt}\cos q_2 = -\sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_2$):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 \ddot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 (\ddot{q}_1 + \ddot{q}_2) + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \ddot{q}_1 \cos q_2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \ddot{q}_2 \cos q_2 - 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \sin q_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2^2 \sin q_2$$

Apply Euler-Lagrange equation:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_1} = \tau_1$$

Substituting and grouping terms in matrix form:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 = & \left[ (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \right] \ddot{q}_1 \\
& + \left[ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \right] \ddot{q}_2 \\
& - 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \sin q_2 - m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2^2 \sin q_2 \\
& + (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation for $q_2$

Following the same procedure:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \sin q_2 - m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_2} = m_2 l_2^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \cos q_2$$

Taking the time derivative and applying Euler-Lagrange:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 = & \left[ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \right] \ddot{q}_1 + m_2 l_2^2 \ddot{q}_2 \\
& + m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1^2 \sin q_2 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Final Matrix Equation

The closed-form dynamic model is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} C_{11} & C_{12} \\ C_{21} & C_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where:

$$M_{11} = (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$

$$M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$

$$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2$$

$$C_{11} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2 \sin q_2, \quad C_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \sin q_2$$

$$C_{21} = m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \sin q_2, \quad C_{22} = 0$$

$$G_1 = (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$G_2 = m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Tip:** Always check that $M(q)$ is symmetric ($M_{12} = M_{21}$) — this is a guaranteed property of any properly derived Euler-Lagrange model.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Euler-Lagrange Formulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1: Define Generalized Coordinates q1, q2 ... qn"] --> B["STEP 2: Compute Position Vectors of Each Link Center of Mass"]
    B --> C["STEP 3: Compute Velocities by Differentiating Positions"]
    C --> D["STEP 4: Compute Total Kinetic Energy K"]
    B --> E["STEP 4b: Compute Total Potential Energy P"]
    E --> F["STEP 5: Form Lagrangian L = K minus P"]
    D --> F
    F --> G["STEP 6: Apply Euler Lagrange Operator for Each qi"]
    G --> H["STEP 7: Compute Partial Derivative of L with respect to qi"]
    G --> I["STEP 7b: Compute d by dt of Partial L with respect to qi dot"]
    H --> J["STEP 8: Equate Operator to Applied Generalized Force tau i"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["STEP 9: Assemble Closed Form Matrix Equation M q double dot + C q dot + G = tau"]
    K --> L["STEP 10: Verify Symmetry and Positive Definiteness of M"]
    L --> M["OUTPUT: Robot Dynamic Model Ready for Control Design"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation of Lagrangian Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT ENERGY DOMAIN"]
        K["KINETIC ENERGY K : Depends on q dot and q"]
        P["POTENTIAL ENERGY P : Depends on q only"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["LAGRANGIAN PROCESSING"]
        L["LAGRANGIAN L = K minus P"]
        EL["EULER LAGRANGE OPERATOR : d by dt of partial L by partial q dot minus partial L by partial q"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT EQUATIONS OF MOTION"]
        EQ["TAU = M q double dot plus C q dot plus G q"]
        M["M q : Inertia Effects"]
        C["C q dot : Coriolis and Centrifugal Effects"]
        G["G q : Gravitational Effects"]
    end
    K --> L
    P --> L
    L --> EL
    EL --> EQ
    EQ --> M
    EQ --> C
    EQ --> G
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparison of Force Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TAU["Generalized Force tau : Input from Robot Actuators"]
    TAU --> M_term["Inertia Torque M q double dot"]
    TAU --> C_term["Coupling Torque C q dot : Velocity Dependent"]
    TAU --> G_term["Gravity Torque G q : Configuration Dependent"]
    M_term --> M1["Dominates during High Acceleration"]
    C_term --> C1["Dominates during High Speed Motion"]
    G_term --> G1["Always Present : Independent of Velocity"]
    M1 --> CTRL["FEEDBACK TO: Computed Torque Controller"]
    C1 --> CTRL
    G1 --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three physical effects (inertia, Coriolis, gravity) arise *automatically* from the energy-based formulation. In contrast, deriving them via Newton's method requires explicit free-body diagrams and projection of forces at every joint — a process that becomes exponentially harder as DOF increases.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Euler-Lagrange equation. Explain the physical significance of the Lagrangian $L$ in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Euler-Lagrange equation is a second-order differential equation derived from **Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action**. For a robotic manipulator with generalized coordinate $q_i$, it is written as:
>
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$
>
> where $L = K - P$ is the **Lagrangian**, $K$ is the total kinetic energy, and $P$ is the total potential energy of the manipulator.
>
> **Physical Significance:** The Lagrangian represents the **net available mechanical energy** for motion. Its derivatives have specific physical meanings:
> - $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ — the **generalized momentum** conjugate to $q_i$.
> - $\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ — the **generalized force** arising from potential gradients (gravity).
>
> In robotics, this formulation eliminates the need to compute internal joint reaction forces, making it highly suitable for deriving the closed-form dynamic model $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ for multi-DOF manipulators.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three properties of the mass (inertia) matrix $M(q)$ obtained from the Euler-Lagrange formulation. Why are these properties important for control?**

**Model Answer:**

The inertia matrix $M(q)$ obtained from the Euler-Lagrange formulation possesses the following properties:

1. **Symmetry:** $M(q) = M^T(q)$ — guaranteed by the energy-based derivation.

2. **Positive Definiteness:** $x^T M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ — ensures that kinetic energy is always non-negative.

3. **Boundedness:** $m_1 \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq x^T M(q) x \leq m_2 \Vert x \Vert^2$, where $0 < m_1 \leq m_2 < \infty$ are constants — ensures uniform bounds across all configurations.

**Importance for Control:** These properties are essential for proving the **global asymptotic stability** of controllers like Computed Torque Control using **Lyapunov's direct method**. Specifically, $M(q)$ being positive definite allows the Lyapunov function candidate $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ to be a valid energy-like positive definite function whose derivative can be made negative definite, guaranteeing tracking error convergence to zero.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Derive the Euler-Lagrange equation from Hamilton's principle. Using this equation, derive the equations of motion for a simple pendulum of mass $m$ and length $l$. Assume the pivot is fixed. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Euler-Lagrange Equation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Consider a system with one generalized coordinate $q(t)$. The action integral is:

$$\mathcal{S} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} L(q, \dot{q}, t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Consider a varied path $q(t) + \epsilon \eta(t)$ where $\epsilon$ is small and $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$.

**Step 3:** Impose stationarity: $\left. \frac{d\mathcal{S}}{d\epsilon} \right\vert_{\epsilon=0} = 0$.

$$\frac{d\mathcal{S}}{d\epsilon} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} \eta + \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \right] dt = 0$$

**Step 4:** Apply integration by parts:

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} \dot{\eta} \, dt = -\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \eta \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary:

$$\boxed{\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = 0}$$

#### Part (b) — Application to Simple Pendulum (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Generalized coordinate: $q = \theta$ (angle from vertical).

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy:

$$K = \frac{1}{2} m (l \dot{\theta})^2 = \frac{1}{2} m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2$$

**Step 3:** Potential energy (taking datum at the lowest point):

$$P = m g l (1 - \cos \theta)$$

**Step 4:** Lagrangian:

$$L = K - P = \frac{1}{2} m l^2 \dot{\theta}^2 - m g l (1 - \cos \theta)$$

**Step 5:** Compute partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta} = -m g l \sin \theta$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m l^2 \dot{\theta}$$

**Step 6:** Take time derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}}\right) = m l^2 \ddot{\theta}$$

**Step 7:** Apply Euler-Lagrange equation with applied torque $\tau$:

$$m l^2 \ddot{\theta} + m g l \sin \theta = \tau$$

For the unforced case ($\tau = 0$):

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{l} \sin \theta = 0$$

This is the well-known **nonlinear pendulum equation**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a 2-DOF planar manipulator having link masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ concentrated at the distal ends and link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, derive the complete dynamic model in the standard manipulator form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ using the Euler-Lagrange formulation. Clearly identify the mass matrix, Coriolis matrix, and gravity vector. (14 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Setting Up the Lagrangian (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Generalized coordinates: $q_1, q_2$ — the joint angles of link 1 and link 2 respectively.

**Step 2:** Position vectors (point masses at the end of each link):

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos q_1, \quad y_1 = l_1 \sin q_1$$

$$x_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 3:** Velocities by time differentiation:

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1, \quad \dot{y}_1 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

**Step 4:** Squared speeds:

$$v_1^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2$$

$$v_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2$$

**Step 5:** Kinetic energy:

$$K = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 \left[ l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_2^2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2 \right]$$

**Step 6:** Potential energy:

$$P = (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \sin q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 7:** Lagrangian:

$$L = K - P$$

#### Part (b) — Computing the Three Matrices (7 Marks)

Applying the Euler-Lagrange operator $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ for $i = 1, 2$ and simplifying:

**Mass (Inertia) Matrix:**

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2) l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 \\ m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2 & m_2 l_2^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Coriolis/Centrifugal Matrix:**

$$C(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_2 \sin q_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \sin q_2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 \sin q_2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Gravity Vector:**

$$G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 + m_2) g l_1 \cos q_1 + m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ m_2 g l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final Assembled Dynamic Model:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = M(q) \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + C(q,\dot{q}) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + G(q)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Errors in $C(q,\dot{q})$:** Many students forget that Coriolis terms arise from differentiating $\cos q_2$ (yielding $-\sin q_2 \, \dot{q}_2$). Double-check signs carefully — wrong signs here will cause your mass matrix symmetry check to also fail.
> 2. **Missing the Cross Term:** Forgetting the $2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \cos q_2$ term in $K$ leads to incorrect $M_{11}$ and $M_{12}$ entries.
> 3. **Symmetry Violation:** A non-symmetric $M(q)$ is a red flag for the examiner — verify $M_{12} = M_{21}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Gravity Datum:** Choosing the pivot as datum (instead of the lowest point) introduces unnecessary constants. Always choose the lowest reachable point for simplicity.
> 5. **Skipping Steps in Part (a):** Examiners reward showing intermediate $K$ and $P$ calculations. Skipping directly to the final matrix costs up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Robotics Module 3**

- **Euler-Lagrange Equation (Single DOF):**
  $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = Q$$

- **Euler-Lagrange Equation (Multi-DOF, Vector Form):**
  $$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q} = \tau, \quad q \in \mathbb{R}^n, \tau \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

- **Lagrangian Definition:** $L = K - P$, where $K$ is total kinetic energy and $P$ is total potential energy.

- **Standard Manipulator Form:**
  $$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

- **$M(q)$ Properties:** Symmetric, positive definite, bounded above and below by positive constants.

- **$C(q,\dot{q})$ Properties:** The matrix $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is **skew-symmetric** — a critical property used in Lyapunov stability proofs.

- **$G(q)$ Properties:** Configuration-dependent only; vanishes in microgravity environments.

- **Energy Origin:** Choose the lowest reachable point as the potential energy datum to simplify expressions.

- **Method Comparison:** Newton-Euler is computationally efficient for forward dynamics (recursive); Euler-Lagrange is preferred for deriving analytical, closed-form dynamic models.

- **Control Application:** Computed Torque Control law:
  $$\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

- **Common Exam Mistake:** Confusing **generalized coordinates** with **Cartesian coordinates** — generalized coordinates are independent parameters that fully describe system configuration (e.g., joint angles for a manipulator).

- **Historical Note:** Leonhard Euler derived the foundational form in the 1740s; Joseph-Louis Lagrange generalized it in *Mécanique Analytique* (1788).

- **Exam Tip:** For any n-DOF problem, expect to demonstrate (i) derivation of $K$ and $P$, (ii) computation of at least one full $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ term explicitly, and (iii) final assembled matrix equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Model of Planar and Spatial Serial Robots up to 2 DOF
Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Actuator Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox** is the mathematical formulation of the electromechanical behaviour of a robotic joint actuator — comprising a **DC servomotor** coupled to a **gear reduction unit** driving an inertial load — using differential equations that capture the electrical, mechanical, and coupling dynamics into a unified input–output model (typically a transfer function or state-space equation).

In plain words: When you command a robot joint to move, an **electric motor** spins very fast but with low torque. You cannot connect it directly to a heavy robotic link because the link needs **slow, powerful rotation**. So you insert a **gearbox** between the motor and the link. To design a controller for this joint, you must first write down the **equations of motion** of the motor coils, the spinning shaft, the gears, and the load — all linked together.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Gear Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of a **bicycle**. Your pedalling legs spin the small front sprocket *very fast* (like the motor). The large rear sprocket turns *slowly but powerfully*, driving the wheel (the robot link). The "ratio" between them is the **gear reduction ratio (n)**. The motor–gearbox model simply puts math around this exact physical intuition: small + fast → large + slow, with torques and inertias scaled by **n²**.

### Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Standard Metrics)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **V** | Applied armature voltage | **V (Volts)** |
| **R** | Armature resistance | **Ω (Ohms)** |
| **L** | Armature inductance | **H (Henry)** |
| **i(t)** | Armature current | **A (Ampere)** |
| **J_m** | Motor rotor inertia | **kg·m²** |
| **B_m** | Motor viscous friction | **N·m·s/rad** |
| **K_b** | Back-emf constant | **V·s/rad** |
| **K_t** | Torque constant | **N·m/A** |
| **θ_m** | Motor shaft angle | **rad** |
| **n = N₁/N₂** | Gear reduction ratio (motor : load) | **dimensionless** |
| **J_L** | Load (link) inertia | **kg·m²** |
| **τ_m** | Motor generated torque | **N·m** |
| **τ_L** | Load disturbance torque | **N·m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key identity for SI units:** Numerically, **K_b = K_t** when SI units are used consistently. This is a direct consequence of energy conservation and the KTU board expects students to use this identity freely.

---

## 2. Why Model the Motor and Gearbox at All?

Before deriving anything, the *why* matters for KTU:

1. **Controller design** — Every robotic joint needs a position/velocity controller (PID, state-feedback). Controllers are designed *from the model*, never from intuition alone.
2. **Stability analysis** — Without a model you cannot predict whether the closed-loop system will oscillate or diverge.
3. **Simulation** — Before fabricating hardware, KTU projects require MATLAB/Simulink simulations of the joint, which demand the transfer function.
4. **Gain selection** — The motor + gearbox parameters dictate the maximum achievable **bandwidth** of the joint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem Breakdown

The Motor–Gearbox–Load system is decomposed into **three coupled subsystems**:

### A. Electrical Subsystem (Armature Circuit)

Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop yields:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Equation:**
> $$V(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + K_b\,\omega_m(t)$$

- The applied voltage **V** drops across three elements: **resistor (Ri)**, **inductor (L·di/dt)**, and the **back-emf (K_b·ω_m)** generated because the spinning coil cuts magnetic flux.

### B. Mechanical Subsystem (Motor Shaft)

Newton's Second Law for rotation on the motor shaft:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical Equation:**
> $$J_m\,\frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m\,\omega_m = K_t\,i(t) - \tau_{reflected}$$

- Motor torque **K_t·i** accelerates the rotor inertia **J_m** and overcomes bearing friction **B_m**, with the remaining torque **τ_reflected** transmitted *through the gearbox* to the load.

### C. Gearbox + Load Subsystem

The gearbox is **ideal, massless, and lossless** (standard KTU assumption). Energy conservation across meshing gears gives:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gearbox Coupling Laws:**
> $$\omega_m = n\,\omega_L \quad\quad \theta_m = n\,\theta_L \quad\quad \tau_{reflected} = n\,\tau_L$$

The torque transmitted *back* from load to motor side is **scaled by n** (torque amplification = the whole point of a gearbox).

## 2.2 Reflected Parameters (The Heart of Gearbox Modelling)

Reflecting load quantities to the **motor side** of the gearbox eliminates n from the equations entirely:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reflected Inertia and Friction:**
> $$J_{eq} = J_m + n^2 J_L \quad\quad B_{eq} = B_m + n^2 B_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why n²?** Power = τ·ω is invariant across an ideal gearbox. Equating kinetic energy on both sides yields **½ J_m ω_m² = ½ J_L ω_L²**, and with ω_m = n·ω_L one obtains **J_reflected = n² J_L**. This n² scaling is the **single most-tested fact** in KTU Module 3.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = Ri + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + K_b\omega_m$ | Armature KVL | Time domain |
| 2 | $J_m\dot\omega_m + B_m\omega_m = K_t i - \tau_{ref}$ | Motor shaft dynamics | Time domain |
| 3 | $\omega_m = n\,\omega_L,\;\;\tau_{ref}=n\,\tau_L$ | Gearbox coupling | Kinematic |
| 4 | $J_{eq}=J_m+n^2 J_L,\;\;B_{eq}=B_m+n^2 B_L$ | Reflected parameters | Equivalent |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V(s)}=\dfrac{K_t}{s\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})+K_bK_t\big]}$ | Position TF (motor side) | s-domain |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\theta_L(s)}{V(s)}=\dfrac{K_t/n}{s\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})+K_bK_t\big]}$ | Position TF (load side) | s-domain |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\omega_m(s)}{V(s)}=\dfrac{K_t}{(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})+K_bK_t}$ | Velocity TF (motor side) | s-domain |
| 8 | $K_b = K_t$ | SI-unit identity | Constants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This model is used to size gear ratios (pick n so J_m ≈ n²J_L for **impedance matching** → maximum power transfer), to design joint servocontrollers in industrial robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC all use derived transfer functions), and to simulate robotic manipulator joints in **MATLAB/Simulink** for KTU mini-projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation — From Physical Laws to Transfer Function

We derive the canonical position transfer function $\theta_m(s)/V(s)$ step-by-step with zero shortcuts.

### Step 1 — Write the electrical equation in time domain

Applying KVL around the armature loop:

$$V(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + e_{back}(t)$$

where $e_{back}(t) = K_b\,\omega_m(t)$ is the back-emf.

### Step 2 — Substitute definitions and isolate terms

$$V(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + K_b\,\frac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt}$$

since $\omega_m(t) = d\theta_m/dt$.

### Step 3 — Write the mechanical equation on the motor shaft

Summing torques on the rotor (Newton's 2nd law for rotation):

$$J_m\,\ddot\theta_m(t) + B_m\,\dot\theta_m(t) = \tau_{motor}(t) - \tau_{ref}(t)$$

with motor torque $\tau_{motor} = K_t\,i(t)$.

### Step 4 — Apply gearbox coupling

The load is reflected to the motor side. Define equivalent inertia and damping:

$$J_{eq} = J_m + n^2\,J_L \quad\quad B_{eq} = B_m + n^2\,B_L$$

The reflected load torque is $\tau_{ref} = n\,\tau_L$. If we neglect external load disturbance ($\tau_L = 0$ — a standard KTU assumption for free-motion analysis), the mechanical equation becomes:

$$J_{eq}\,\ddot\theta_m + B_{eq}\,\dot\theta_m = K_t\,i(t)$$

### Step 5 — Take Laplace transforms (zero initial conditions)

Applying $\mathcal{L}\{\,\cdot\,\}$ with zero ICs:

$$V(s) = (Ls + R)\,I(s) + K_b\,s\,\Theta_m(s)$$

$$(J_{eq}s + B_{eq})\,s\,\Theta_m(s) = K_t\,I(s)$$

### Step 6 — Solve the mechanical equation for I(s)

$$I(s) = \frac{s(J_{eq}s + B_{eq})}{K_t}\,\Theta_m(s)$$

### Step 7 — Substitute I(s) into the electrical equation

$$V(s) = (Ls + R)\cdot\frac{s(J_{eq}s + B_{eq})}{K_t}\,\Theta_m(s) + K_b\,s\,\Theta_m(s)$$

Factor out $\Theta_m(s)$:

$$V(s) = s\,\Theta_m(s)\left[\frac{(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})}{K_t} + K_b\right]$$

### Step 8 — Form the position transfer function (motor side)

$$V(s) = s\,\Theta_m(s)\cdot\frac{(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_b K_t}{K_t}$$

Solving for the ratio $\Theta_m(s)/V(s)$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\,\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_b K_t\big]}\;}$$

### Step 9 — Convert to the load-side transfer function

Since $\theta_L = \theta_m/n$:

$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1}{n}\cdot\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t/n}{s\,\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_b K_t\big]}$$

### Step 10 — Velocity transfer function

Differentiate the position numerator by removing the outer '1/s' integration:

$$\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_b K_t}$$

### Step 11 — Standard 2nd-Order Canonical Form

Expanding the denominator:

$$(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) = LJ_{eq}s^2 + (LB_{eq}+RJ_{eq})s + RB_{eq}$$

So the characteristic polynomial is:

$$LJ_{eq}s^2 + (LB_{eq}+RJ_{eq})s + (RB_{eq}+K_b K_t) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Conditions:**
> 1. Gearbox is **ideal** (no backlash, no friction, no compliance).
> 2. **L < J_eq·R/B_eq** such that the system is well-damped and physically realizable.
> 3. **No external load torque** (free motion). Add τ_L as a disturbance input in the extended model.
> 4. **SI units throughout**, so $K_b = K_t$ (a frequent KTU trick question).

---

## 3.2 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A robot joint has a motor with $R=1\,\Omega$, $L=0.5\,H$, $J_m=0.01\,kg\cdot m^2$, $B_m=0.001\,N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$, $K_t=0.05\,N\cdot m/A$. The gearbox ratio is $n=10$, and the link inertia is $J_L=1.0\,kg\cdot m^2$. Find the equivalent inertia and the closed-form position transfer function at the load side.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute reflected inertia:
$$J_{eq} = J_m + n^2 J_L = 0.01 + (10)^2(1.0) = 100.01\;kg\cdot m^2$$

Step 2 — Neglect B_L (assume B_eq ≈ B_m = 0.001):
$$B_{eq} = 0.001\;N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$$

Step 3 — Note $K_b = K_t = 0.05$.

Step 4 — Substitute into the load-side transfer function:
$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{0.05/10}{s\big[(0.5s+1)(100.01s+0.001)+(0.05)(0.05)\big]}$$

Step 5 — Simplify numerator:
$$\text{Numerator} = 0.005$$

Step 6 — Expand denominator inner term:
$$(0.5s+1)(100.01s+0.001) = 50.005s^2 + 0.0005s + 100.01s + 0.001$$
$$= 50.005s^2 + 100.0105s + 0.001$$

Step 7 — Add $K_bK_t$:
$$50.005s^2 + 100.0105s + 0.001 + 0.0025 = 50.005s^2 + 100.0105s + 0.0035$$

Step 8 — Final transfer function:
$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{0.005}{s(50.005s^2 + 100.0105s + 0.0035)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The system is a **Type-1, third-order** system with a pole at the origin (integrator for position), and two real poles very close to the origin (slow mechanical response dominated by the heavy reflected load). This is typical of geardown robotic joints.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Motor–Gearbox–Load Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **signal flow** and **energy coupling** of the modelled system. Node IDs are alphanumeric to satisfy the compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vsrc["V Applied Voltage"] --> ElecSum(("Sum Junction"))
    Emf["Back EMF Kb times omega"] --> ElecSum
    ElecSum --> Armature["Armature Circuit L and R"]
    Armature -->|"Current i(t)"| CurrentNode["i t"]
    CurrentNode --> TorqueGen["Torque Generator Kt times i"]
    TorqueGen --> MechSum(("Sum Junction"))
    Fric["Friction Bm times omega"] --> MechSum
    MechSum --> Inertia["Equivalent Inertia J eq"]
    Inertia -->|"theta m"| Split["Split Node Gearbox"]
    Split -->|"omega m back to Emf"| Emf
    Split -->|"Divide by n"| LoadOut["theta L Output"]
    LoadOut --> Reflect["Reflected Load n squared times J L"]
    Reflect --> MechSum
    classDef elec fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000;
    classDef mech fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000;
    classDef coup fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000;
    class Vsrc,Emf,ElecSum,Armature,CurrentNode elec;
    class TorqueGen,MechSum,Fric,Inertia,LoadOut mech;
    class Split,Reflect coup;
```

## 4.2 Annotated Interpretation of the Diagram

| Block | Subsystem | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|
| **V Applied Voltage** | Input | External command signal |
| **Armature Circuit (L, R)** | Electrical | $V - K_b\omega = Ri + L\,di/dt$ |
| **Torque Generator (K_t·i)** | Electro-mechanical coupling | Lorentz force law |
| **Equivalent Inertia (J_eq)** | Mechanical | $J_{eq}\ddot\theta_m = K_t i - B_{eq}\dot\theta_m$ |
| **Friction (B_m·ω)** | Mechanical damping | Viscous bearing drag |
| **Back-EMF (K_b·ω)** | Mechano-electrical coupling | Faraday's law |
| **Split Node (÷ n)** | Gearbox kinematic relation | $\theta_L = \theta_m/n$ |
| **Reflected Load (n²·J_L)** | Energy conservation | Kinetic-energy invariance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Two **feedback loops** are visible. The **inner loop** is electrical (back-emf opposes applied voltage — motor braking effect). The **outer loop** is mechanical (reflected load inertia acts back on the motor shaft). Both loops must be present in any KTU block-diagram question worth full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the electromechanical coupling equations for a DC servomotor and define each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DC servomotor is described by two coupled first-order differential equations:

**(i) Electrical (Armature) Equation:**
$$V(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + K_b\,\omega_m(t)$$

- **V(t)** — applied armature voltage in Volts
- **R** — armature resistance in Ohms
- **L** — armature inductance in Henry
- **i(t)** — armature current in Amperes
- **K_b** — back-emf constant in V·s/rad
- **ω_m(t)** — motor angular velocity in rad/s

**(ii) Mechanical (Shaft) Equation:**
$$J_m\,\frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m\,\omega_m = K_t\,i(t) - \tau_L(t)$$

- **J_m** — rotor moment of inertia in kg·m²
- **B_m** — viscous friction coefficient in N·m·s/rad
- **K_t** — torque constant in N·m/A
- **τ_L(t)** — load torque reflected to motor shaft

**Electromechanical coupling** is established by the term $K_b\omega_m$ in the electrical equation and $K_t i$ in the mechanical equation — energy conversion between electrical and mechanical domains. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the concept of reflecting the load inertia through an ideal gearbox. Why is the reflected inertia scaled by n²?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When a gear train of ratio $n = \omega_m/\omega_L$ couples the motor to a load, the load inertia $J_L$ seen **at the motor shaft** is increased due to the gear reduction. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation using power invariance** for an ideal (lossless) gearbox:

Equating kinetic energy on both sides:
$$\frac{1}{2}J_m\omega_m^2 = \frac{1}{2}J_L\omega_L^2$$

Using $\omega_m = n\omega_L$:
$$J_{reflected}\,(n\omega_L)^2 = J_L\,\omega_L^2 \;\Rightarrow\; J_{reflected} = n^2 J_L$$

The equivalent inertia at the motor shaft is $J_{eq} = J_m + n^2 J_L$. **(2 marks)**

**Physical reason:** The gearbox reduces speed by factor *n* and amplifies torque by the same factor *n*. Since kinetic energy $½J\omega^2$ must be conserved, and $\omega$ is reduced by *n*, the equivalent *J* must increase by **n²**. This is the same principle by which a bicycle's rear wheel becomes "harder" to spin in a higher gear.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function relating the applied armature voltage $V(s)$ to the motor shaft angular position $\theta_m(s)$ for a DC motor coupled to a load through an ideal gearbox of ratio $n$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servomotor has the following specifications: $R=2\,\Omega$, $L=0.1\,H$, $J_m=0.005\,kg\cdot m^2$, $B_m=0.002\,N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$, $K_t=0.1\,N\cdot m/A$, and $K_b=0.1\,V\cdot s/rad$. It drives a load of inertia $J_L=2\,kg\cdot m^2$ through a gear reduction of ratio $n=20$. Determine:
  (i) the equivalent inertia referred to the motor shaft,
  (ii) the open-loop transfer function $\theta_m(s)/V(s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The DC motor has a constant field flux (separately excited or permanent magnet).
2. The gearbox is ideal — no backlash, massless, lossless.
3. The armature reaction is neglected.
4. All parameters are linear and time-invariant (LTI).
5. No external load disturbance torque.

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** KVL around armature circuit:
$$V(t) = R\,i(t) + L\frac{di(t)}{dt} + K_b\omega_m(t) \quad\quad(1)$$

**Step 2:** Newton's 2nd law for the motor shaft (with reflected load torque):
$$J_{eq}\frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_{eq}\,\omega_m = K_t\,i(t) \quad\quad(2)$$

where $J_{eq}=J_m+n^2J_L$ and $B_{eq}=B_m+n^2B_L$.

**Step 3:** Take Laplace transform of (1) with zero initial conditions:
$$V(s) = (Ls+R)\,I(s) + K_b\,s\,\Theta_m(s) \quad\quad(3)$$

**Step 4:** Take Laplace transform of (2):
$$s(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})\,\Theta_m(s) = K_t\,I(s) \quad\quad(4)$$

**Step 5:** Solve (4) for $I(s)$:
$$I(s) = \frac{s(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})}{K_t}\,\Theta_m(s) \quad\quad(5)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute (5) into (3):
$$V(s) = (Ls+R)\cdot\frac{s(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})}{K_t}\,\Theta_m(s) + K_b s\,\Theta_m(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor and rearrange:
$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_bK_t\big]} \quad\quad(6)$$

**Step 8:** Expand denominator to reveal 2nd-order canonical form:
$$(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq}) + K_bK_t = LJ_{eq}s^2 + (LB_{eq}+RJ_{eq})s + (RB_{eq}+K_bK_t)$$

Hence, the standard form is:
$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s\big[LJ_{eq}s^2 + (LB_{eq}+RJ_{eq})s + (RB_{eq}+K_bK_t)\big]}$$

This is a **Type-1, third-order** system. **(1 mark for identifying type & order)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Equivalent Inertia (3 marks):**
$$J_{eq} = J_m + n^2 J_L = 0.005 + (20)^2(2) = 0.005 + 800 = 800.005\;kg\cdot m^2$$

Neglecting $B_L$:
$$B_{eq} \approx B_m = 0.002\;N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$$

**(ii) Open-loop Transfer Function (4 marks):**

Substitute numerical values into equation (6):

- $L=0.1$, $R=2$, $J_{eq}=800.005$, $B_{eq}=0.002$, $K_bK_t=(0.1)(0.1)=0.01$

Numerator:
$$K_t = 0.1$$

Inner denominator term:
$$(0.1s+2)(800.005s+0.002) = 80.0005s^2 + 0.0002s + 1600.01s + 0.004$$
$$= 80.0005s^2 + 1600.0102s + 0.004$$

Add $K_bK_t = 0.01$:
$$80.0005s^2 + 1600.0102s + 0.014$$

Final transfer function:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{0.1}{s\,(80.0005s^2 + 1600.0102s + 0.014)}\;}$$

**System classification (bonus 0.5 marks):** Type-1, order-3 system with very low natural frequency due to large reflected load inertia — characteristic of high-geardown robot joints.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the electromechanical modelling of a separately excited DC motor driving a robotic joint. Clearly mark the electrical and mechanical feedback loops. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic joint uses a DC motor with $R=1\,\Omega$, $L=0.01\,H$, $K_t=0.05\,N\cdot m/A$, $K_b=0.05\,V\cdot s/rad$, $J_m=2\times10^{-4}\,kg\cdot m^2$, $B_m=1\times10^{-4}\,N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$, and a gearbox of ratio $n=50$ driving a load of $J_L=0.5\,kg\cdot m^2$. Obtain:
  (i) the velocity transfer function $\omega_m(s)/V(s)$, and
  (ii) the steady-state velocity for an applied voltage of $V=10\,V$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram. The verbal explanation should cover:

1. **Input** $V(s)$ enters a summing junction. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Electrical subsystem:** $(Ls+R)$ block converts voltage difference to current $I(s)$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Electromechanical coupling 1:** Current $I(s)$ passes through torque constant $K_t$ to produce motor torque $\tau_m(s)$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Mechanical subsystem:** Torque drives the equivalent inertia $J_{eq}s + B_{eq}$ block to produce angular velocity $\omega_m(s)$. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Electromechanical coupling 2:** Velocity $\omega_m$ feeds back through back-EMF constant $K_b$ into the electrical summing junction — this is the **inner electrical feedback loop**. **(1.5 marks)**
6. **Gearbox + load:** The motor velocity is divided by *n* to produce load velocity $\omega_L$, and load inertia $n^2J_L$ is reflected back to the mechanical summing junction — this is the **outer mechanical feedback path**. **(1 mark)**
7. **Output:** $\theta_m(s)$ is the integrated velocity — integrator $1/s$ block. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Velocity Transfer Function (4 marks):**

Compute equivalent inertia:
$$J_{eq} = J_m + n^2 J_L = 2\times10^{-4} + (50)^2(0.5) = 2\times10^{-4} + 1250 = 1250.0002\;kg\cdot m^2$$

Neglect $B_L$:
$$B_{eq} \approx B_m = 1\times10^{-4}\;N\cdot m\cdot s/rad$$

Substitute into velocity TF:
$$\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})+K_bK_t}$$

Plug in numbers:
- $L=0.01$, $R=1$, $J_{eq}=1250.0002$, $B_{eq}=10^{-4}$, $K_bK_t=2.5\times10^{-3}$

Inner product:
$$(0.01s+1)(1250.0002s+10^{-4}) = 12.5s^2 + 10^{-6}s + 1250s + 10^{-4}$$
$$= 12.5s^2 + 1250s + 10^{-4}$$

Add $K_bK_t$:
$$12.5s^2 + 1250s + (10^{-4}+2.5\times10^{-3}) = 12.5s^2 + 1250s + 2.6\times10^{-3}$$

Final TF:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Omega_m(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{0.05}{12.5s^2 + 1250s + 0.0026}\;}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Velocity (3 marks):**

Apply Final Value Theorem with a step input $V(s) = 10/s$:
$$\omega_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0}\, s\cdot\Omega_m(s) = \lim_{s\to 0}\, s\cdot\frac{0.05}{12.5s^2+1250s+0.0026}\cdot\frac{10}{s}$$

$$\omega_{ss} = \frac{0.05\times10}{0.0026} = \frac{0.5}{0.0026} \approx 192.3\;rad/s$$

Convert to rpm (optional): $192.3\times 60/(2\pi)\approx 1836\,rpm$.

**Interpretation:** The motor spins very fast (192 rad/s) at the shaft, but the load side spins at $\omega_L = \omega_m/n = 192.3/50 = 3.85\,rad/s$ — exactly the gear reduction effect. **(bonus 0.5 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to reflect J_L to motor side** — the single biggest mark-loser. Always write $J_{eq}=J_m+n^2J_L$ **before** substituting into any transfer function. **(Lose up to 3 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing motor-side and load-side transfer functions.** The factor of $1/n$ in the load-side TF is often missed. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 3. **Mixing SI and CGS units.** KTU strictly uses SI; using $K_b$ in V/rpm and $K_t$ in oz-in/A without conversion loses full marks.
> 4. **Dropping the back-EMF term $K_b\omega_m$** from the electrical equation. The closed-loop behaviour changes drastically without it.
> 5. **Not identifying the system type and order** at the end of derivation — KTU explicitly tests this awareness.
> 6. **Sign errors** in the summing junctions during block-diagram questions. Verify that back-EMF *subtracts* from applied voltage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Points to Memorize Before the Exam**
>
> - [x] **Two governing equations**: Electrical (KVL on armature) and Mechanical (Newton's 2nd law on shaft).
> - [x] **Electromechanical coupling**: $K_t i$ in the mechanical equation, $K_b\omega$ in the electrical equation.
> - [x] **SI-unit identity**: $K_b = K_t$ when SI units are used throughout.
> - [x] **Gearbox kinematic relations**: $\omega_m = n\omega_L$, $\theta_m = n\theta_L$, $\tau_{ref}=n\tau_L$.
> - [x] **Reflected inertia scaling**: $J_{ref}=n^2 J_L$ — derived from kinetic-energy invariance.
> - [x] **Equivalent parameters**: $J_{eq}=J_m+n^2J_L$, $B_{eq}=B_m+n^2B_L$.
> - [x] **Position TF (motor side)**: $\dfrac{\Theta_m(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s\big[(Ls+R)(J_{eq}s+B_{eq})+K_bK_t\big]}$.
> - [x] **Position TF (load side)**: Divide motor-side TF by $n$.
> - [x] **Velocity TF**: Drop the outer $1/s$ integration.
> - [x] **System is Type-1, order-3** — one integrator (position) + two mechanical poles.
> - [x] **Two feedback loops**: Inner (electrical, back-emf) and outer (mechanical, reflected load).
> - [x] **Assumption checklist**: Ideal gearbox, constant field, linear LTI, no load disturbance.
> - [x] **Impedance matching rule of thumb**: Choose $n$ such that $J_m \approx n^2 J_L$ for maximum power transfer.
> - [x] **Final Value Theorem** gives steady-state velocity: $\omega_{ss} = K_t V / (RB_{eq}+K_bK_t)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET748 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Control Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: The Control Problem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Control Problem in Robotics** is defined as the formulation and design of control laws (algorithms) that drive a robotic manipulator from an initial state to a desired final state, ensuring trajectory tracking, stability, robustness against disturbances, and acceptable performance in terms of speed, accuracy, and overshoot.

Mathematically, given the **nonlinear dynamic equation** of a serial n-DOF manipulator:

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \boldsymbol{\tau}_{dist}$$

the **control problem** reduces to designing the joint torque vector $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ such that the actual joint trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ asymptotically tracks (or converges to) the desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_{d}(t)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – Driving a Car on a Curved Road:**
> Imagine you are driving a car. Your brain continuously compares the **actual position** of the car on the road with the **desired position** (the lane centre). The difference is called the *error*. Based on this error, your hands rotate the steering wheel (apply *control action*) to reduce the error. If the road curves sharply, you turn more (proportional to error). If the car is swerving fast, you counter-steer quickly (proportional to rate of error). This is exactly what a **PID controller** does in a robot — the *Proportional* term steers, the *Derivative* term damps the swerve, and the *Integral* term eliminates steady drift.

### 1.3 Why "The Control Problem" is a Challenge

A robot manipulator is, fundamentally, a **multi-input multi-output (MIMO)**, **nonlinear**, **coupled**, and **time-varying** dynamical system.

| Property | Consequence in Control |
|---|---|
| **Nonlinearity** | Inertia, Coriolis, and gravity terms depend on configuration $\mathbf{q}$ |
| **Coupling** | Motion of joint $i$ affects all other joints |
| **Time-varying** | Mass matrix $\mathbf{M(q)}$ changes as the arm moves |
| **MIMO nature** | Scalar PID design is not directly applicable |
| **Uncertainty** | Payload, friction, and external forces are unknown |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 Emphasis: The question on "The Control Problem" almost always begins with the dynamic equation $\mathbf{M(q)q̈ + C(q,q̇)q̇ + G(q) = τ}$ and then asks to derive a control law. Memorize this equation and its components.

### 1.4 Categories of the Control Problem

1. **Regulation (Point-to-Point) Control:** Drive the manipulator from any initial configuration to a fixed desired configuration and hold it there.
2. **Trajectory Tracking Control:** Force the manipulator to follow a time-varying reference trajectory $\mathbf{q}_{d}(t)$.
3. **Force Control:** Regulate the contact force when the end-effector interacts with the environment.
4. **Hybrid Position/Force Control:** Simultaneously control position in unconstrained directions and force in constrained directions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Control Architecture

The robot control architecture is conventionally split into **three hierarchical layers**:

- **High-Level (Task Planning):** Defines *what* the robot should do (path planning, task sequencing).
- **Mid-Level (Trajectory Generation):** Converts task description into time-indexed reference $\mathbf{q}_{d}(t)$.
- **Low-Level (Servo Control):** Computes joint torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ so that $\mathbf{q}(t) \to \mathbf{q}_{d}(t)$. This is the heart of *The Control Problem*.

## 2.2 Sources of Nonlinearity — Step-by-Step

1. **Inertia (Configuration-Dependent):** The moment of inertia seen by each joint changes as the arm extends or folds. Encapsulated in the **mass matrix** $\mathbf{M(q)}$.
2. **Coriolis & Centrifugal Forces:** Caused by the rotation of links. Encapsulated in the matrix $\mathbf{C(q,q̇)}$, multiplying $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
3. **Gravity Loading:** Each joint must support the weight of the links beyond it. Encapsulated in the vector $\mathbf{G(q)}$.
4. **Friction:** Stiction (static) + Coulomb + viscous friction. Often lumped into $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{f}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.
5. **External Disturbances:** $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{dist}$ from contact, payload variation, or unmodelled dynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Why** behind these terms: A robot arm is a chain of rigid bodies connected by revolute or prismatic joints. Newton's second law applied to each link, after substituting kinematic relations (Jacobian, velocity, acceleration), gives this compact second-order nonlinear ODE.

## 2.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control

| Aspect | Open-Loop (Non-Servo) | Closed-Loop (Servo) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (uses $\mathbf{q}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$) |
| **Accuracy** | Low; depends on calibration | High; corrects errors continuously |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed (no feedback loop) | Must be designed carefully |
| **Examples** | Pick-and-place with hard stops, CNC drilling | Welding, painting, assembly, walking |
| **KTU Tag** | *Bang-Bang* control | *PID, Computed Torque, Adaptive* |

## 2.4 Control Law Classification (KTU High-Yield)

### (a) Decentralized (Independent Joint) Control
Each joint is treated as an independent servomotor. Coupling and nonlinearities are treated as **disturbances**.

$$\tau_{i} = K_{p,i} \, e_{i}(t) + K_{d,i} \, \dot{e}_{i}(t) + K_{i,i} \int e_{i}(t)\, dt + \hat{G}_{i}(\mathbf{q})$$

where $e_{i}(t) = q_{d,i}(t) - q_{i}(t)$ is the tracking error at joint $i$, and $\hat{G}_{i}$ is a feed-forward gravity-compensation term.

### (b) Centralized (Model-Based) Control
Uses the **full nonlinear dynamic model** of the robot to compute exact torques.

$$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,[\ddot{\mathbf{q}}_{d} + \mathbf{K}_{v}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e}] + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

This is the famous **Computed Torque Control (CTC)** law. After substitution, the closed-loop error dynamics become **decoupled linear second-order systems**:

$$\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{v}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The eigenvalues of $\mathbf{K}_{p}$ and $\mathbf{K}_{v}$ can be assigned arbitrarily (provided they are positive definite), making this a **Pole-Placement** problem in joint space.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M(q)q̈ + C(q,q̇)q̇ + G(q) = τ}$ | Full nonlinear dynamic model | Starting point of every control design |
| 2 | $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{q}_{d} - \mathbf{q}$, $\dot{\mathbf{e}} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}_{d} - \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Tracking error definitions | Used in all feedback laws |
| 3 | $\mathbf{τ} = \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{K}_{d}\dot{\mathbf{e}}$ | PD control (joint space) | Decentralized regulation |
| 4 | $\mathbf{τ}_{PD} = \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e} + \mathbf{K}_{d}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{G(q)}$ | PD + Gravity compensation | Regulation under gravity load |
| 5 | $\mathbf{τ}_{CTC} = \mathbf{M(q)[q̈}_{d} + \mathbf{K}_{v}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e}] + \mathbf{C(q,q̇)q̇ + G(q)}$ | Computed Torque Control | Trajectory tracking |
| 6 | $\ddot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{v}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$ | Decoupled error dynamics (CTC) | Gain tuning via pole placement |
| 7 | $\mathbf{K}_{p} = \text{diag}(\omega_{n,i}^{2})$, $\mathbf{K}_{v} = \text{diag}(2\zeta_{i}\omega_{n,i})$ | Critical-damped choice | Standard CTC tuning |
| 8 | $\mathbf{V} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{e}}^{T}\mathbf{M(q)}\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{e}^{T}\mathbf{K}_{p}\mathbf{e}$ | Lyapunov function candidate | Stability proof |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{V}} = -\dot{\mathbf{e}}^{T}\mathbf{K}_{d}\dot{\mathbf{e}} \leq 0$ | Lyapunov derivative (PD) | Asymptotic stability proof |
| 10 | $\mathbf{J(q)}\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}$, $\mathbf{x}$: Cartesian | Velocity kinematics | Task-space control |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, all $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation that might appear in determinants or norms should be written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ (without breaking the table). The KTU formula sheet is **evaluative** — missing a subscript like $d$ for *desired* loses a full mark.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Welding Robots:** Use trajectory tracking (CTC variant) because the seam must be followed at constant speed.
- **Space Manipulators (Canadarm):** Use adaptive control because payload inertia is unknown.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use force/impedance control to protect tissue.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas):** Use Model Predictive Control (MPC) layered on top of CTC.
